meta.name: VerifyWise AI App Trust & Transparency Index
meta.scopeStatement: Each grade reflects our analysis of what an app states in its public privacy policy and terms as of the assessment date. It measures the transparency of those documents, not the company's actual data practices, security, or compliance. Grades are our opinion, offered for general information.
meta.disclaimer: The VerifyWise AI Trust & Transparency Index is provided for general informational purposes only and reflects VerifyWise's good-faith analysis of publicly available privacy policies and terms of service as of the stated assessment date. Grades are opinions based on a published methodology, not statements of fact, legal advice, or an endorsement or warning about any company or product. We do not audit, test, or verify any company's actual data handling, security, or legal compliance. These documents change over time, and an assessment may contain errors or omissions. VerifyWise makes no warranty as to accuracy or completeness and accepts no liability for decisions made in reliance on the index. Company and product names are trademarks of their respective owners; their inclusion does not imply any affiliation or endorsement. To request a correction, contact privacy@verifywise.ai.
meta.rankingSource: a16z Top 100 Gen AI Consumer Apps, 6th Edition (Mar 2026)
meta.enterpriseRankingSource: Menlo Ventures: 2025 State of Generative AI in the Enterprise (Dec 2025)
meta.methodologyNote: Apps are scored across 7 data-governance domains (training-data use, data-subject rights, retention, third-party sharing, transparency, sensitive data & children, security & accountability) on a 10-point additive scale, scaled to 100. Every score is anchored to a quoted clause or a recorded silence. An app with an active dealbreaker clause (sells data with no opt-out, claims a perpetual content licence, or refuses deletion) is capped at a displayed B.

apps.name: Claude
apps.summary: Claude's policy is a detailed data governance disclosure. It grants the full set of data-subject rights, assigns ownership of Outputs to the user, names international transfer safeguards, and confirms it does not sell personal data. Two limitations keep the grade in B: Inputs and Outputs are used to train models by default unless the user opts out through account settings, and data retention is described only as lasting as long as reasonably necessary, with no general day count.

highlights.label: Trains by default with opt-out
highlights.text: The policy says Anthropic may use Inputs and Outputs to train its AI models unless the user opts out through account settings, which caps the training indicator at half and keeps the grade in B. Feedback and safety-flagged content stay in scope even after opting out.

highlights.label: User owns outputs
highlights.text: The consumer terms say Anthropic assigns to the user all of its rights, title, and interest in Outputs, and the user retains rights in Inputs. There is no broad content licence concern.

highlights.label: Full rights with a 30-day deletion path
highlights.text: Section 4 grants access, portability, deletion, correction, and objection. Deleted conversations are removed immediately from history and automatically deleted from the back-end within 30 days.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards but vague retention
highlights.text: International transfers rely on named adequacy decisions and standard contractual clauses under Articles 45 and 46 GDPR. Data retention is stated only as lasting as long as reasonably necessary, with no general day count.

apps.name: ChatGPT
apps.summary: OpenAI's privacy policy earns ChatGPT a solid B. It names a training opt-out, grants a full set of data-subject rights routed through a real portal and DPO, states it does not sell personal data, offers an advertising opt-out, and commits to removing deleted data within 30 days. The score is held back by generic security language with no named encryption standard, no named international transfer safeguard such as SCCs or adequacy, and silence on output ownership (the terms of use that would cover it failed to capture in the snapshot) and on breach notification. The grade keeps its full range because no dealbreaker rights are reserved.

highlights.label: Training opt-out
highlights.text: Content may be used to train the models that power ChatGPT. The data controls let you choose whether your Content can be used to improve and train the models, with a linked instruction page for opting out.

highlights.label: 30-day deletion
highlights.text: After you choose to delete Personal Data, the policy says it is removed from systems within 30 days. Temporary Chats are deleted automatically within 30 days.

highlights.label: Full rights set
highlights.text: The Your rights section grants access, rectification, deletion, restriction, portability, and consent withdrawal. You can exercise them through privacy.openai.com or dsar@openai.com, with a named DPO at dpo@openai.com.

highlights.label: Does not sell, ads sharing has opt-out
highlights.text: The policy states it does not sell Personal Data. Targeted advertising sharing for Free and Go users can be turned off via the marketing privacy control or Global Privacy Control.

apps.name: Perplexity
apps.summary: Perplexity's policy is strong on data-subject rights, transparency, and international transfers. It trains on search data by default but offers a named opt-out for AI model improvement, which earns partial training credit and keeps the app in the B band. The disclosure also covers deletion within 30 days, a stated no-sale position with an opt-out for ad sharing, named transfer safeguards, and a named DPO.

highlights.label: Training opt-out
highlights.text: The policy gives a named control. If you are logged in, you can opt out of information collection for AI on your settings page, which prohibits use of your search information to improve the AI models. Because training is on by default, this earns partial credit.

highlights.label: Deletion in 30 days
highlights.text: Users can request account deletion through the settings page or by emailing support. The policy states that if you delete your account, it aims to delete your personal information from its servers within 30 days.

highlights.label: Does not sell, shares for ads with opt-out
highlights.text: Perplexity states it does not sell personal information. It does share data for targeted advertising and provides a Do Not Share link plus recognition of Global Privacy Control signals as a valid opt-out.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: For UK and EEA users the policy relies on the European Commission standard contractual clauses and certifies to the EU-US Data Privacy Framework and the UK Extension, with adequacy decisions where applicable.

apps.name: Gemini
apps.summary: Gemini adds strong data-subject rights, retention controls, a named training control, and a clear statement that data is not sold to advertisers on top of a detailed Google Privacy Policy. By default, chats are used to improve the service, with a Keep Activity setting and a past-chats control available to limit that, which puts the app at the train-by-default ceiling. The Gemini-specific answers in this snapshot are collapsed to headings, so the app-level training, retention, and AI-log details are scored from the parent policy plus the visible headings. Output ownership is silent here.

highlights.label: Named training control
highlights.text: The notice exposes a Keep Activity setting and a control titled How can I control whether Gemini Apps use my past chats to personalize my experience. This gives users a named way to limit how chats are used to improve the service.

highlights.label: Does not sell to advertisers
highlights.text: The Google Privacy Policy states We don't share information that personally identifies you with advertisers and We don't show you personalized ads based on your content from Drive, Gmail, or Photos, so data is not sold for ads.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: My Activity, Export your data, and the Delete your information flow let users access, port, and delete data, and the notice adds a heading on how to object to processing or ask for inaccurate data to be corrected.

highlights.label: International transfer safeguards stated generically
highlights.text: The Data transfers section says information may be processed on servers outside the country where you live and that Google complies with certain legal frameworks, but it names no specific safeguard such as SCCs or adequacy in this snapshot.

apps.name: Meta AI
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: Under Developing and improving AI at Meta the policy lists Public content you or others create or share and Interactions with AI at Meta and related metadata as data used to develop and improve AI at Meta, and this snapshot names no mechanism to opt those interactions out of training.
apps.summary: Meta AI runs on Meta's full privacy policy plus the Meta AIs Terms of Service, and the disclosures are detailed. Users get a strong rights slate (access in a portable format, deletion, and correction through the Privacy Center) and an explicit no-sell promise. Training is the main weakness: the policy says outright that public content and interactions with AI at Meta are used to develop and improve AI at Meta, and this snapshot names no opt-out for that use. Retention is vague too, decided case by case with no day count and no AI-specific deletion timeline, and the transfer-safeguard and security sections appear only as headers, with no SCCs, adequacy, or named controls. Because the training disclosure is an explicit adverse reservation, the dealbreaker flag is raised and the displayed grade is capped at B, though the number already sits below that at 62.

highlights.label: Trains on interactions with no opt-out shown
highlights.text: The Developing and improving AI at Meta section lists public content and interactions with AI at Meta and related metadata as inputs to develop and improve AI at Meta, and this snapshot names no way to opt those interactions out of training.

highlights.label: Full slate of user rights
highlights.text: The AI Terms grant the right to request access to personal information in a portable format, deletion, and correction, backed by Privacy Center tools to access, port, download, or delete your information.

highlights.label: Explicit no-sell promise
highlights.text: The policy states We don't sell any of your information to anyone, and we never will, though information is still shared widely for ad personalization across the Meta Companies.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague
highlights.text: The policy says We keep information as long as we need it and We decide how long we need information on a case-by-case basis, with no day count and no AI-specific deletion timeline.

changes.value: D1.1: Under Developing and improving AI at Meta the policy lists Public content you or others create or share and Interactions with AI at Meta and related metadata as data used to develop and improve AI at Meta, and this snapshot names no mechanism to opt those interactions out of training.

apps.name: Canva
apps.summary: Canva's privacy policy is detailed and well organized. It grants the standard data-subject rights through a named contact, names its cross-border transfer safeguards, and gives users a clear way to opt out of AI training. The score is held to a B because Canva trains on user activity, content, and media uploads by default, its retention language stays vague with no day-count or deletion timeline for general users, and it says nothing about who owns AI outputs or how synthetic content is marked.

highlights.label: AI training opt-out is named
highlights.text: Section 2 says Canva may analyze your activity, content, and media uploads to train its algorithms, models, and AI products, and Sections 7(d) and 2 let you manage this in the privacy controls and AI personalization settings. Canva Education User Content is excluded from AI training entirely.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights with a contact
highlights.text: Section 7(e) grants access, correction, deletion or restriction, a structured electronic copy, and objection, all exercisable by emailing privacy@canva.com. Section 12 adds a 'do not sell or share' opt-out for US state-law residents.

highlights.label: Named cross-border transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 11(c) commits to the EU Model Clauses and the UK International Data Transfer Addendum for transfers to countries without an adequacy decision, and Section 5 cites adherence to the EU-US, Swiss-US, and UK Data Privacy Framework.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague
highlights.text: Section 8 says Canva keeps profile information and User Content 'for a commercially reasonable time, and for as long as we have a valid purpose to do so,' with no day-count or deletion timeline for general users. Canva Education has a defined 12-month plus 3-month inactive-account schedule.

apps.name: ElevenLabs
apps.summary: ElevenLabs publishes a detailed privacy policy with a named training opt-out, strong international transfer safeguards, and explicit biometric governance. Three gaps keep it out of the A band: it trains on user data by default, it pairs a vague general retention standard with a 3 year cap on voice data, and it says nothing about breach notification or synthetic audio marking. Training by default with an opt-out caps the grade in the B band. The score holds up against the ChatGPT calibration anchor: ElevenLabs gives more on transfer and biometric disclosures, less on deletion-timeline specifics, and has no breach notification clause.

highlights.label: Named training opt-out
highlights.text: The policy trains models on user data by default but provides a specific opt-out. Users open the Data use menu in the Terms and Privacy section of their account. The opt-out applies only to data provided after the request is submitted.

highlights.label: Strong transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers name concrete mechanisms: EU Standard Contractual Clauses, Brazil Standard Contractual Clauses, adequacy decisions, and certification to the EU-US, Swiss-US and UK Data Privacy Framework.

highlights.label: Biometric data governed and capped
highlights.text: Voice Data may constitute biometric data and is collected for verification. It is retained until it is no longer needed or 3 years after the relationship ends, whichever comes first.

highlights.label: Sale and sharing carries an opt-out
highlights.text: Sharing through targeting cookies counts as a sale or sharing under US state laws. Users can opt out via Modify Cookies in the footer, and Global Privacy Control signals are honored.

apps.name: Character.AI
dealbreakerFlags: Trains AI/ML models on user content with no disclosed opt-out (D1.1 adverse)
apps.summary: Character.AI lists full data-subject rights, but it names model training as a use of collected content and gives no opt-out for it in the policy. That explicit training reservation triggers a dealbreaker and pulls the score into the D band. Retention, deletion timelines, breach notification, sub-processors, and security certifications are all unaddressed.

highlights.label: Trains on content, no opt-out
highlights.text: Section 2 lists uses including "to train our artificial intelligence/machine learning models" and to "Develop new features, algorithms and machine learning models," while Your Choices only offers an opt out of "sales of personal information or targeted advertising," so there is no stated way to refuse training.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: Section 4 grants access and a copy "in a portable and/or machine-readable format," the right "to update or correct," "to request that we delete certain personal information," "to object to how we process," and account deletion through the profile page.

highlights.label: Retention is vague
highlights.text: Section 7 says "We keep your information for the time necessary for the purposes for which it is processed... unless a shorter retention period is required by law," with no day count or deletion timeline, and it reserves the right to keep popular Characters active even after account deletion.

highlights.label: Children gate and sensitive-data limit
highlights.text: Section 6 states the Services are "not designed for minors under 13" and prohibit use under 13, or under 16 in the EEA or UK, and Section 1 says "Sensitive personal information is not required or necessary to use the Services - please do not include any sensitive personal information."

changes.value: Trains AI/ML models on user content with no disclosed opt-out (D1.1 adverse)

apps.name: DeepSeek
apps.summary: DeepSeek discloses a full set of data-subject rights and a training opt-out. It still trains on user inputs by default, keeps data for vague "as long as necessary" periods, and says nothing about output ownership, breach notification, or named security and transfer safeguards. The grade of 64 (C) fits the policy: it gets credit for strong rights and an explicit no-sale clause, and loses points for training by default, vague retention, generic security language, and an unnamed cross-border transfer safeguard. It ranks below ChatGPT (76, B) because it gives no concrete deletion timeline and names no transfer mechanism.

highlights.label: Trains by default with opt-out
highlights.text: The policy lists the right to opt out of using your Personal Data for training our models or optimizing our technologies. Inputs feed model training unless the user acts, which caps this indicator at half credit.

highlights.label: Strong rights, weak retention
highlights.text: The EEA section grants access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection, and restriction. Retention is described only as long as necessary, with deletion tied to when data is no longer required and no day count given.

highlights.label: Does not sell personal data
highlights.text: The policy states in capitals that it does not engage in targeted advertising, does not sell personal data, and does not use it for profiling. This earns full credit on the sell-or-share indicator.

highlights.label: Data stored in China with unnamed safeguards
highlights.text: Data is collected, processed, and stored in the People's Republic of China. Cross-border transfers rely only on appropriate safeguards in accordance with applicable data protection laws, with no SCCs, adequacy decision, or DPF named.

apps.name: Grok
apps.summary: Trains by default with no general opt-out, and no 'we don't sell' statement.

highlights.label: Training
highlights.text: Trains by default; only Google-OAuth content excluded. Private Chat deletes in 30 days.

highlights.label: Rights
highlights.text: Access, correct, update, delete; portability not explicitly named.

highlights.label: Selling
highlights.text: No explicit 'we do not sell' in the main policy.

highlights.label: Children
highlights.text: Under-13 gate with 13–17 parental supervision.

apps.name: Janitor AI
apps.summary: Janitor AI's policy has a full data-subject rights chapter and names EU standard contractual clauses for cross-border transfers. It does not say whether chat content trains its AI models, and it gives no concrete retention or breach-notification timelines.

highlights.label: Rights covered
highlights.text: Section 9 grants access, a machine-readable copy of your data, correction, deletion through account settings, restriction, objection, and consent withdrawal, with a contact route at support@janitorai.com.

highlights.label: Training use unaddressed
highlights.text: The policy mentions conducting research to improve its AI systems but never states whether user chat content is used for model training, and it offers no training opt-out. The training indicators score zero on silence.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguard
highlights.text: Section 10 commits to standard contractual clauses approved by relevant authorities for cross-border transfers, and Section 15 states the company does not sell personal data under the CCPA and CPRA.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no breach clock
highlights.text: Section 8 keeps data only for as long as necessary with no day-count. The Section 11 security clause names encryption and access controls but sets no breach-notification timeframe.

apps.name: Leonardo.Ai
apps.summary: Strong data rights and opt-out controls for training and ads, with named transfer safeguards; the gap is no synthetic-image marking.

highlights.label: Training
highlights.text: Free content is trained on by default; paid subscribers opt out via private mode, and tier differences are disclosed.

highlights.label: Rights
highlights.text: Access, deletion, portability, correction and objection, plus an opt-out of ad sharing and sales.

highlights.label: Transfers
highlights.text: EU Model Clauses and the UK International Data Transfer Addendum named; adequacy referenced.

highlights.label: Transparency gap
highlights.text: The policy does not say generated images are AI-created or commit to marking synthetic output.

apps.name: Notion
apps.summary: Notion discloses its data governance: by default it does not train on customer data, users own their AI inputs and outputs, AI-log retention is given as concrete numbers, and the certification stack is broad. The weaker areas are vague general retention language, no formal mapping of legal bases to purposes, and ad-related data sharing, with a named opt-out and honoring of global privacy controls.

highlights.label: No training by default
highlights.text: The AI security page states that by default Notion and its AI subprocessors do not use customer data to train any models, with contractual prohibitions on subprocessors and no license granted to Notion to train on customer data.

highlights.label: You own your AI content
highlights.text: The policy states Notion does not claim ownership of your input or generated output, and that input and output are your customer data under the Notion AI supplementary terms.

highlights.label: Concrete AI retention numbers
highlights.text: Enterprise workspaces get zero data retention with LLM providers, non-Enterprise data is retained 30 days or fewer, embeddings are deleted within 60 days, and deleted pages become unrecoverable after 30 days.

highlights.label: Named security certifications
highlights.text: The security page names SOC 2 Type 2, ISO 27001, 27701, 27017 and 27018, HIPAA, and BSI C5, plus TLS 1.2 or greater for data sent to AI subprocessors.

apps.name: Photoroom
apps.summary: Photoroom posts a detailed GDPR and CCPA privacy policy with a full per-purpose processing table, named retention periods, a working account-level training opt-out, and a declared external DPO. That puts it in the B band, behind the assistant leaders. Because the policy trains uploaded images by default on a legitimate-interest basis with an opt-out, D1.1 lands at half and the score caps in the B range.

highlights.label: Training opt-out is named
highlights.text: The policy states Photoroom uses uploaded images to train its models on a legitimate-interest basis and that you can opt out at any time by changing the settings on your account, with API images excluded from model improvement.

highlights.label: Concrete retention numbers
highlights.text: Retention is set out per purpose with day counts. Account data is kept until deletion or 2 years from last connection then archived 5 years, activity data is deleted 30 days after a deletion request, and refund data is kept 6 months.

highlights.label: Does not sell or share
highlights.text: The California notice says Photoroom does not sell or share personal data as those terms are defined by the CCPA, and that it does not disclose personal information to third parties for their own direct marketing.

highlights.label: Full rights set plus a declared DPO
highlights.text: The policy grants GDPR access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, and objection rights with a one-month response window. It names an external Data Protection Officer declared to the CNIL, reachable at privacy@photoroom.com.

apps.name: Grammarly
apps.summary: Grammarly's policy, now published under the Superhuman name after the corporate rebrand, meets GDPR standards. It grants the full set of data-subject rights, maps purposes to legal bases, and names its international transfer safeguards. Its weak spots: it trains AI on user content by default behind a thinly described account setting, and it gives no concrete retention periods, deletion timelines, or breach notification details. Those gaps hold it at the bottom of the B band.

highlights.label: Trains by default with an opt-out
highlights.text: The policy says it uses user content to train its AI models and that you can decide whether your content is used by adjusting the training control in account settings. That control is a named opt-out, but the default is to train, which holds the grade to roughly a B.

highlights.label: Strong data-subject rights
highlights.text: It grants access, deletion, correction, portability, objection, and restriction rights, honors Global Privacy Control signals, and offers a targeted-advertising opt-out through the Your Privacy Choices link in the footer.

highlights.label: Named international safeguards
highlights.text: It certifies to the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks and relies on standard contractual clauses for transfers to other countries.

highlights.label: Weak retention and breach disclosure
highlights.text: Retention is described only as long as necessary, with no day count and no deletion timeline. The security section names encryption and access minimization but says nothing about breach notification.

apps.name: Kling AI
apps.summary: Kling AI discloses a wide set of data-subject rights along with its sharing and transparency practices, but it does not say whether user content is used to train its models, which keeps it in the C band. The policy is the genuine Kling AI Pte. Ltd (Kuaishou) document for klingai.com, and every applicable indicator resolves to a quoted clause or documented silence, so the grade rests on adequate evidence.

highlights.label: Training use undisclosed
highlights.text: The policy never states whether uploaded photos, videos, or generated content feed model training. Section 2 lists ten purposes including product development, analytics, content moderation, and marketing, with no training purpose and no opt-out, so all four D1 indicators score zero on silence. Because this is silence and not an adverse reservation, no dealbreaker flag applies.

highlights.label: Wide set of user rights
highlights.text: Section 8 grants access and a copy, rectification, deletion, portability in a structured machine-readable format, and the right to object. It names an in-app account deletion path via [My Space] - [Click on the head portrait] - [Account Deletion].

highlights.label: Does not sell personal data
highlights.text: The California section states the company does not sell consumers' personal data unless it obtains consent, and it provides an opt-out of any future sales, which meets the no-sale standard.

highlights.label: Concrete retention only in the Korea section
highlights.text: The general retention clause is the vague as long as necessary. The South Korea terms give numbers including 3 months for internet log and access-location data and 5 years for contract and payment records, which anchors the retention and deletion-timeline credit.

apps.name: FaceApp
apps.summary: FaceApp earns a solid B. It limits uploaded photos and videos to the editing functionality, deletes them from cloud servers within 24 to 48 hours of the last edit, encrypts each file with a key stored locally on the device, and states it does not sell personal information. Users keep ownership of their content, and FaceApp's license is limited to providing the service. The policy is silent on breach notification, on governing the biometric facial data it processes, and on special-category data limits, which keeps it out of A territory.

highlights.label: Photos used only for editing
highlights.text: The policy states FaceApp uses photographs or videos you provide only to provide the editing functionality, and each selected file is encrypted with a key stored locally on your device.

highlights.label: Short, concrete retention
highlights.text: Photographs and videos and their associated information are deleted within 24 to 48 hours after the last edit, with a Request cloud data removal button to delete sooner.

highlights.label: No sale and named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: FaceApp states it does not sell personal information and names Standard Contractual Clauses and the UK International Data Transfer Agreement as safeguards for cross-border transfers.

highlights.label: Silent on breach and biometric governance
highlights.text: The security section offers only commercially reasonable security practices with no breach-notification commitment, and despite facial transformation processing there is no clause governing biometric or special-category data.

changes.value: Terms grant FaceApp a perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide licence to use and create derivative works from any user feedback, for any purpose.

apps.name: Microsoft Copilot
apps.summary: Microsoft Copilot is governed by the Microsoft Privacy Statement, which includes an Artificial Intelligence and Copilot section. That section gives Copilot a named training opt-out, since conversation data trains the generative models in certain markets unless the user opts out, and it points to the Microsoft Privacy Dashboard for managing prompt history. The statement names the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks as its cross-border transfer mechanism and names a Data Protection Officer with a contact route. The consumer statement says nothing about retention periods, deletion timelines, portability, rectification, or breach notification, which keeps the score in the C band. The grade uses the correct governing policy, more than the required share of indicators is evidenced, and no harmful right is reserved, so no dealbreaker applies.

highlights.label: Trains by default with opt-out
highlights.text: In certain markets, conversation data trains the generative AI models in Copilot unless the user opts out, and prompt history can be managed in product and on the Microsoft Privacy Dashboard. This is train-by-default with an opt-out, which caps the indicator at half and keeps the app below the top band.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguard
highlights.text: Microsoft names the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks as its cross-border transfer mechanism, which satisfies the international transfer safeguard indicator.

highlights.label: Retention left unstated
highlights.text: The statement only promises a later section on how long data is retained and never gives a retention period or a deletion timeline as a number, so the retention indicators score zero.

highlights.label: Control tooling and named DPO
highlights.text: Access and clearing of data run through the Microsoft Privacy Dashboard, the advertising opt-out runs through a dedicated opt-out page, and a Data Protection Officer is named with a contact route.

apps.name: Midjourney
dealbreakerFlags: Trains on user inputs (prompts, uploaded photos and images) with no opt-out mechanism disclosed (D1.1)
apps.summary: Midjourney discloses strong data-subject rights and a named international transfer safeguard, but it explicitly collects user prompts and uploaded images for model training with no training opt-out anywhere in the policy, which caps it in the C band. The dealbreaker rests on an affirmative disclosure: the policy states it collects data through the process of training its machine learning algorithms and lists prompts and uploaded images as collected data. Output ownership is silent because the linked Terms of Service page returned a 404, so it was scored as a gap. Retention and security are vague, and there is no breach-notification clause, named certification, or DPO.

highlights.label: Trains on inputs, no opt-out
highlights.text: The policy states it collects personal data through the process of training Midjourney machine learning algorithms and lists prompts and uploaded photos and images as collected data. The only opt-outs offered cover marketing cookies and sale or sharing, with no training opt-out anywhere in the document.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: Both the EEA and California sections grant access, correction, deletion, portability, and objection, with a named exercise path at midjourney.com/account and a 45-day response window for California requests.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguard
highlights.text: For EEA, Switzerland and UK transfers, the policy relies on the European Commission-approved standard contractual clauses plus additional safeguards where appropriate.

highlights.label: Vague retention and thin security
highlights.text: Retention is stated only as long as is necessary and security as commercially acceptable means. There is no breach-notification clause and no certification or DPO named.

changes.value: Trains on user inputs (prompts, uploaded photos and images) with no opt-out mechanism disclosed (D1.1)
Users grant Midjourney a perpetual, irrevocable, sublicensable licence to reproduce and distribute their outputs for any purpose.

apps.name: Civitai
apps.summary: Civitai runs on a thin, pre-AI-era privacy policy (last updated February 1, 2024). It describes data collection and sharing reasonably well but says nothing about model training, retention periods, deletion timelines, or formal data-subject rights, which puts it deep in the F band. Its good disclosures are limited to sharing and transparency. The gaps around training, retention, rights, and breach notification are silent rather than adverse, so no dealbreaker flags apply.

highlights.label: Silent on training
highlights.text: The policy never says whether user-submitted content or images are used to train AI models. It describes data use only as to provide, maintain, improve, and enhance its services and to develop new products, services, and features.

highlights.label: No retention or deletion timeline
highlights.text: The policy states no retention period, deletion timeline, or data minimization commitment, so every retention indicator is unresolved.

highlights.label: No formal rights
highlights.text: The Your Choices section offers only sharing preferences and a promotional email unsubscribe link. It states no right to access, delete, port, or correct personal data.

highlights.label: Consent-only international transfers
highlights.text: For cross-border data the policy relies only on consent, stating that by providing any information, including personal information, on or to the Services, you consent to such transfer. It names no SCCs, adequacy decision, or DPF safeguard.

apps.name: Pixelcut
apps.summary: Pixelcut runs a standard auto-generated privacy policy that grants a full set of data-subject rights across GDPR, US state laws, and Brazil. The policy says nothing about AI model training, synthetic-output marking, retention periods, or breach notification. The rights coverage is strong, but for an image-generation tool the document never discloses that it uses AI, and every training and output-ownership question goes unanswered, which holds the score in the D band.

highlights.label: Strong rights coverage
highlights.text: The policy spells out access, deletion, portability, rectification, and objection across GDPR, twenty US states, and Brazil, and it honors the Global Privacy Control without friction.

highlights.label: Silent on training
highlights.text: Nothing in the policy addresses whether uploaded images or other inputs are used to train models, so there is no opt-out and no tier distinction to evaluate.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no breach notice
highlights.text: Data is kept for as long as the collection purpose requires, with no day-count, and the document has no breach notification clause anywhere.

highlights.label: Transfers rest on consent
highlights.text: EU and UK transfers rely on explicit user consent after a warning about the absence of an adequacy decision and appropriate safeguards. The policy does not name standard contractual clauses, adequacy, or the Data Privacy Framework.

apps.name: Freepik
apps.summary: Freepik's AI services run on the Magnific privacy policy, a GDPR-based document with a dedicated AI Suite section. That section states that under no circumstances will it use uploaded user images or voices, or those of third parties, to train or improve its own or third-party AI models, which earns a full training-data score. Uploaded inputs are deleted immediately after the output is generated, sub-processor model providers retain data for a maximum of 30 days, and the policy grants all data-subject rights, including a copy within one month and erasure by closing the account. It names Standard Contractual Clauses under Article 46.2.c GDPR as the transfer safeguard, names a Data Protection Officer, and describes SSL encryption for data in transit. Two gaps hold it back from an A: it is silent on breach notification timing and on watermarking of synthetic output, and a California disclosure says it may sell or share identifiers and network activity for advertising, subject to an opt-out.

highlights.label: No training on user content
highlights.text: The AI Suite section states that under no circumstances will Freepik use uploaded images or voices, or those of third parties that the user uploads, to train or improve its own AI models or those of third-party providers, which earns a full training-data score.

highlights.label: Short input retention
highlights.text: Images and audio files uploaded by the user are deleted immediately after the synthetic content is generated, and sub-processor model providers retain the data for a maximum of 30 days after the output is produced.

highlights.label: Complete rights set with timeline
highlights.text: The policy grants access with a copy provided within one month, rectification, erasure by closing the account, portability, restriction, and objection, covering all five data-subject rights.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguard and ad sharing with opt-out
highlights.text: Freepik enters into Standard Contractual Clauses under Article 46.2.c GDPR with all third-party model providers, while the California notice says it may sell or share identifiers and network activity for advertising, subject to an opt-out.

apps.name: Picsart
apps.summary: Picsart documents EU and UK data-subject rights, names standard contractual clauses for international transfers, and lists a data protection officer. The policy says nothing about whether images users upload or generate are used to train its AI models, gives no training opt-out, and reserves the right to keep some data indefinitely. Those governance basics alongside the open training and retention questions place it in the middle of the C band.

highlights.label: Full EU/UK rights set
highlights.text: EU and UK residents can ask Picsart to confirm processing, access, correct, delete, restrict, and receive their data in machine readable format. Picsart commits to a one month response window and charges nothing for these requests.

highlights.label: Training use undisclosed
highlights.text: The policy never states whether images that users upload or generate feed AI model training, and it provides no training opt-out. For an image app, this leaves the core question unanswered.

highlights.label: Retention runs long and open-ended
highlights.text: Picsart keeps most data for as long as the account is active, holds certain data for up to 3 years after closure, and reserves the right to store some information indefinitely where terms were violated or to improve its services.

highlights.label: Biometric face scanning with partial controls
highlights.text: Picsart scans face geometry from uploaded images as biometric data. TrueDepth API data stays on the device, is not persistently stored, and is not shared with third parties. Broader biometric consent and retention limits are not spelled out.

apps.name: Higgsfield
dealbreakerFlags: Trains on user content with no opt-out: section 2.3 states it trains its algorithms on user-shared text and multimedia data and query and prompt data, and the policy names no training opt-out mechanism (only a general GDPR objection right for European users)
apps.summary: Higgsfield's privacy policy is structured and GDPR-aware. It grants the full set of European rights, maps its legal bases, and names safeguards for international transfers. Two problems pull the score down: section 2.3 trains on user content with no opt-out, and the policy is thin on general retention and security detail. That puts it in the C band, with the training clause as an active dealbreaker.

highlights.label: Trains on your content, no opt-out
highlights.text: Section 2.3 says it improves the Service by using your user-shared text and multimedia data and your query and prompt data to train its algorithms, and the policy names no way to opt out of training.

highlights.label: Full European rights set
highlights.text: Section 14.1 grants access, correction, deletion, portability, restriction, objection, and consent withdrawal, exercisable by emailing support@higgsfield.ai with a one-month response target.

highlights.label: Concrete content deletion timelines
highlights.text: Section 5.9 states removed content may stay on active servers for 30 days and that a cancelled account is purged within 90 days, which is more specific than most peers.

highlights.label: Named international transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 15.2 names the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, adequacy decisions, and Standard Contractual Clauses as the mechanisms used for transfers outside Europe.

changes.value: Trains on user content with no opt-out: section 2.3 states it trains its algorithms on user-shared text and multimedia data and query and prompt data, and the policy names no training opt-out mechanism (only a general GDPR objection right for European users)

apps.name: Ideogram
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: training data use with no opt-out mechanism disclosed
apps.summary: Ideogram lists a full slate of data-subject rights and says it does not sell or share personal information. It also trains its models on user input with no opt-out anywhere, gives only vague retention language, and says nothing about output ownership, deletion timelines, breach notification, or security specifics. Training by default with no opt-out is an explicit adverse term, which triggers the training dealbreaker flag.

highlights.label: Trains on user input with no opt-out
highlights.text: The policy names "training the models that power our Services" as a use of submitted information, and the only opt-outs offered cover marketing emails and cookies. The policy also states it does not honor opt-out preference signals, so there is no way to opt out of training. This raises the training dealbreaker flag.

highlights.label: Does not sell or share
highlights.text: The policy states "We do not 'sell' or 'share' (as those terms are defined under the CCPA) personal information, nor have we done so in the preceding 12 months," the strongest disclosure in the document.

highlights.label: Full slate of rights through a named channel
highlights.text: Access, deletion, correction, portability, restriction, and objection are all listed, and you exercise them by emailing privacy@ideogram.ai. A denied request has an appeal route.

highlights.label: Vague retention and no deletion timeline
highlights.text: Retention is described only as "for as long as is reasonably necessary for the purposes specified," with no day count and no stated deletion timeframe.

changes.value: D1.1: training data use with no opt-out mechanism disclosed

apps.name: Manus
apps.summary: Manus discloses a GDPR-grade set of data-subject rights, legal-basis mappings, and international-transfer safeguards. It says nothing about whether user inputs train any model, so it scores a C.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: The European notice grants access, correction, deletion, portability, restriction, objection, and consent withdrawal. Deletion is framed as removal where there is no good reason to keep processing.

highlights.label: Training use left unstated
highlights.text: The policy lists service improvement, analytics, and new product development as legitimate-interest uses of inputs. It never says inputs are used to train an AI model and offers no training opt-out, so the training domain scores zero on silence.

highlights.label: Concrete sandbox retention
highlights.text: Sandbox virtual-machine data is kept for up to 7 days from last activity on free plans and up to 14 days on paid plans, then automatically deleted. That gives a named retention number and a tiered log window.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards plus DPO
highlights.text: International transfers rely on adequacy decisions and standard-form contracts approved by the relevant authorities. EU and UK GDPR representatives (EDPO) are named, and a Data Protection Officer has been appointed at privacy@manus.im.

apps.name: QuillBot
apps.summary: QuillBot's policy is the Learneo umbrella privacy policy, which explicitly carves out QuillBot for AI training. It grants strong regional data-subject rights and names concrete international transfer safeguards. By default it trains its AI models on QuillBot user text inputs under legitimate interest, and users get only a general GDPR objection right, with no dedicated training opt-out. All concrete retention periods and deletion timelines are deferred to a product-specific Part Two section that is not present in the captured policy, so those commitments cannot be verified.

highlights.label: Trains on inputs by default
highlights.text: The EEA legitimate-interest table lists training or improving the AI models that power our Services with personal data categories that include User Text Inputs and Content (QuillBot only). QuillBot user text feeds model training by default. Users get only a general GDPR objection right, with no dedicated training opt-out.

highlights.label: Full regional rights
highlights.text: The EEA and UK section grants access, rectification, erasure, portability in a machine-readable format, restriction, and objection. The CCPA and other US state sections add deletion and the right to opt out of sale and of sharing for cross-context behavioral advertising.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: The Cross Border Transfers section names the EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks, European Commission adequacy decisions, and Standard Contractual Clauses for transfers to countries without an adequacy decision.

highlights.label: Retention deferred, no numbers
highlights.text: The Data Retention section says only that personal data is retained as described in the applicable Part Two section. No concrete retention period, deletion timeline, or breach-notification commitment appears in the captured policy.

apps.name: Kimi
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: User Content (prompts, files, audio, images) is used for training and optimizing models with no opt-out offered in the policy
apps.summary: Kimi grants named data-subject rights and maps processing purposes to legal bases. It also states that user content is used to train and optimize its models by default and names no opt-out mechanism anywhere in the policy. That training reservation holds the grade in the C band. Retention has no day-count and breach notification has no fixed deadline.

highlights.label: Trains on user content by default
highlights.text: User content including prompts, audio, images, videos and files is processed to provide and improve the Services, including training and optimizing models. No opt-out mechanism is named anywhere in the policy.

highlights.label: Full slate of rights
highlights.text: Access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection and consent withdrawal are each spelled out, exercisable through account settings or membership@moonshot.ai.

highlights.label: Does not sell personal data
highlights.text: The sharing section opens with a statement that Kimi does not sell personal information, and sub-processors are listed by category under a contractual instruction-only limit.

highlights.label: Vague retention and breach timing
highlights.text: Retention is kept only as long as necessary with no day-count, and breach response is described as immediate notification by telephone or push without a fixed deadline.

changes.value: D1.1: User Content (prompts, files, audio, images) is used for training and optimizing models with no opt-out offered in the policy
Trains on user content (prompts, audio, images, videos, files) by default with no opt-out named in the policy.

apps.name: Mistral Le Chat
apps.summary: Mistral's GDPR-grounded privacy policy covers user rights, retention numbers, and a named training opt-out, but it trains Le Chat Input and Output by default and says nothing about output ownership, breach notification, or a children age gate, which keeps it at the top of band B. Every highlighted claim was confirmed in the snapshot: the training opt-out and the Enterprise and paid-API carve-out in Section 3, the day-count retention ladder in Section 5, the U.S. no-sale and no-targeted-ads disclosure in Section 9, and the Article 46 Standard Contractual Clauses commitment in Section 7. No certification was fabricated and no dealbreaker was raised on silence. The score sits above the ChatGPT anchor of 76 because Mistral gives named account controls for access, deletion, portability, and training objection, plus a named DPO. Training by default caps it at B.

highlights.label: Named training opt-out
highlights.text: The policy says Mistral uses Input and Output to train its models subject to your opt-out, and that it does not train on Le Chat Enterprise or paid API usage. Section 8 gives a user control to object to training directly from your account.

highlights.label: Concrete retention numbers
highlights.text: Section 5 sets day-count retention: Input and Output kept until you delete the conversation or account, API data kept 30 rolling days for abuse monitoring unless zero data retention is on, account data 1 year, and civil identity 5 years.

highlights.label: Does not sell or run targeted ads
highlights.text: The U.S. disclosures state Mistral has not sold, shared, or run targeted advertising with consumer personal data in the preceding 12 months, including for users known to be under 18.

highlights.label: International transfers with named safeguard
highlights.text: Section 7 commits to Article 46 GDPR safeguards and attaches the European Commission Standard Contractual Clauses to every contract where a provider processes data outside the EU.

apps.name: SeaArt
apps.summary: SeaArt's 2023 privacy policy sets out data-subject rights in detail but says nothing about whether it trains models on user images, who owns outputs, or how long data is kept. It scores in the D band.

highlights.label: Strong rights coverage
highlights.text: The policy grants access, portability, correction, erasure, restriction, and objection rights, each routed to customer@seaart.ai, with a one-month response window.

highlights.label: Silent on training
highlights.text: Nothing in the policy says whether user prompts or generated images feed model training, and there is no named opt-out, so the training-data domain scores zero.

highlights.label: Vague retention
highlights.text: Data is kept until the end of its life cycle with no day count, and deletion happens only in accordance with local laws. The policy gives no concrete retention commitment.

highlights.label: Generic security, no breach notice
highlights.text: Security is described only as administrative, physical, and technical measures with no named controls, and there is no breach-notification clause or certification claim.

apps.name: Talkie
apps.summary: Talkie's policy grants a full set of data-subject rights and gives clear security and legal-basis disclosures. It does not say whether conversations train shared models, and its retention language is vague, which places it in the middle of band C.

highlights.label: Full rights slate
highlights.text: Section 7.3 grants access, a portable machine-readable copy, correction, deletion via account settings, consent withdrawal, and the right to object and restrict processing.

highlights.label: Conversations walled off from ads
highlights.text: The policy states it will never share Talkie conversations, photos, or other content with advertising partners or use them for marketing, and the CCPA table marks message content as 'We do not sell/share'.

highlights.label: Vague retention only
highlights.text: Section 6 commits to retaining data 'only as long as necessary' with no day-count and no deletion timeline, and the Singapore section adds only 'as soon as it is reasonable'.

highlights.label: Named security and DPO
highlights.text: Section 4 cites encryption in transit, firewalls, and role-based access controls, and the regional sections name a Data Protection Officer at a registered Singapore address.

apps.name: Cutout.Pro
apps.summary: Cutout.Pro has a real but thin privacy policy. It earns most of its points on short image retention and clear no-training and no-sharing commitments. It says nothing about data portability, rectification, breach notification, certifications, children, or policy versioning. The policy reserves no harmful right, so there are no dealbreaker flags, and the low score comes from how much it leaves unaddressed.

highlights.label: Strong short retention
highlights.text: Uploaded photos stay cached on AWS for only 24 to 48 hours after the last edit, server logs are kept no longer than 3 months, and uploaded images are deleted automatically. The policy gives concrete retention numbers for both content and logs.

highlights.label: No training on uploads
highlights.text: The policy states it does not use the photos you provide for any reason other than to provide the portrait editing functionality, so user images are not used for model training.

highlights.label: No third-party sharing or sale
highlights.text: Sharing or publishing information to any third parties is prohibited and no other transmission to third parties takes place. Disclosure to authorities is limited to the event of a hacking attack.

highlights.label: Thin on rights and accountability
highlights.text: Account access and self-service deletion run through the dashboard. The policy says nothing about portability, rectification, breach notification, security certifications, a DPO, children's age gating, or any version or effective date.

apps.name: Hypic
dealbreakerFlags: Trains its machine learning models on user data with no disclosed opt-out (D1.1 adverse)
apps.summary: Hypic discloses strong data-subject rights across many jurisdictions, names its international-transfer safeguards, and protects face data well. But it trains its machine learning models on user data and offers no opt-out anywhere in the policy. That adverse reservation raises a dealbreaker flag and holds the score in the C band.

highlights.label: Trains with no opt-out
highlights.text: The use list includes training and improving its technology, such as machine learning models and algorithms. No training opt-out appears in the main policy or any jurisdiction-specific section. The only opt-outs cover cookies, marketing, and overseas transfers, so training data use is adverse.

highlights.label: Strong face-data handling
highlights.text: The policy says any images collected to provide editing features are deleted promptly after the feature runs and not retained, and that it does not use face or body information to identify people. This earns full marks on biometric governance and shorter AI-processing retention.

highlights.label: Full rights and named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Users can access, delete, rectify, port, and object to processing across the EEA, Brazil, Korea, and other supplements. The European section relies on adequacy decisions and standard contractual clauses for transfers to the United States and Singapore.

highlights.label: Vague retention default
highlights.text: The global retention rule says only that information is kept for as long as necessary to provide the Platform. Concrete periods appear only in the Korea and Indonesia supplements, so the default day-count anchor stays vague.

changes.value: Trains its machine learning models on user data with no disclosed opt-out (D1.1 adverse)
Trains its machine learning models on user content by default, with no opt-out disclosed in the policy.

apps.name: Facemoji
apps.summary: Facemoji's keyboard privacy notice grants a full set of GDPR and California rights with named mechanisms, and it bounds retention to thirty days after the data is no longer needed, with translation text deleted after seven days. The same notice collects biometric and typed data, sells data for interest-based advertising under an opt-out, and leaves biometric handling without a stated consent or destruction schedule. Those gaps, plus a silent stance on output ownership and a training opt-out for typed data that is only a settings toggle, place it at the top of the C band.

highlights.label: Strong rights set
highlights.text: The California and EEA supplements grant access, deletion, portability, rectification, and objection. They include a structured machine-readable export and a Do Not Sell or Share My Information link for opting out of sales.

highlights.label: Bounded retention
highlights.text: Personal data is kept only as long as needed plus thirty days, after which it is deleted or anonymized. Text submitted for translation is stored for just seven days before deletion.

highlights.label: Biometrics collected but ungoverned
highlights.text: The California table marks Biometric Information such as face, eye, or other biometric scans as collected, and facial recognition appears as a stated use. No consent step, retention period, or destruction schedule is set out for this category.

highlights.label: Sells data with an opt-out
highlights.text: The notice states the company may sell personal information to third parties subject to a right to opt out. The sale is disclosed, and there is no commitment not to sell.

apps.name: Gamma
dealbreakerFlags: Trains AI models on user data with no opt-out offered: the research-and-development section lists 'to train our artificial intelligence models' as a use, the European legal-bases table grounds it in legitimate interest, and no training opt-out appears anywhere in the policy.
apps.summary: Gamma's policy grants a strong European data-rights set and names a cross-border transfer safeguard. It also trains its AI models on user content with no opt-out and says nothing about retention periods, output ownership, or breach notification. The training-with-no-opt-out reservation is an explicit adverse term rather than mere silence, so the dealbreaker flag is sound. The score sits in the D band because near-zero training and retention domains pull down otherwise solid rights, sharing, transparency, and sensitive-data coverage.

highlights.label: Trains on your content, no opt-out
highlights.text: The research-and-development section says Gamma may use personal information to train its artificial intelligence models, and the European legal-bases table grounds this in legitimate interest. No training opt-out is offered anywhere in the policy, so user-generated content can feed model training by default.

highlights.label: Full European rights set
highlights.text: The Notice to European Users lists access, correction, deletion, transfer of a machine-readable copy, restriction, objection including direct marketing, and withdrawal of consent, all exercisable by email to privacy@gamma.app, plus the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority.

highlights.label: Named cross-border safeguard
highlights.text: For data leaving Europe the policy relies on adequacy decisions or standard-form contracts approved by relevant authorities, which act as standard contractual clauses, and it notes the U.S. is not the subject of an adequacy decision.

highlights.label: No retention number and vague security
highlights.text: Retention is framed only as keeping data to fulfill the purposes for which it was collected, with no fixed number. Security is described as technical, organizational, and physical safeguards with no specific controls named, and there is no breach-notification commitment.

changes.value: Trains AI models on user data with no opt-out offered: the research-and-development section lists 'to train our artificial intelligence models' as a use, the European legal-bases table grounds it in legitimate interest, and no training opt-out appears anywhere in the policy.
Uses personal information to train its AI models with no opt-out disclosed in the policy.

apps.name: Candy.AI
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: Inputs (user prompts and outputs) are used to train EverAI's AI and moderation models on a legitimate-interest basis with no training opt-out offered
apps.summary: Candy.AI ships a GDPR-grade notice from EverAI Limited that gives users a full set of data-subject rights, a defined retention schedule, and a 30-day purge of interaction log files. By default it trains its AI and moderation models on user chats on a legitimate-interest basis with no opt-out named. That default is a dealbreaker, so it caps the displayed grade and the app lands in C.

highlights.label: Trains on chats by default
highlights.text: Section 2 lists training of AI and moderation models, with dataset preparation that may include human review of de-identified interactions, all on a legitimate-interest basis. No training opt-out is named, so user conversations feed model training by default.

highlights.label: Full GDPR rights menu
highlights.text: Section 9 grants access under Art. 15, rectification, erasure, restriction, and portability in a structured machine-readable form, plus consent withdrawal and the right to object to legitimate-interest processing.

highlights.label: Short log retention and prompt deletion
highlights.text: Log files that record user interactions, user-IDs, and timestamps are automatically deleted after 30 days, and a deletion request removes account data without undue delay.

highlights.label: Does not sell and gates marketing sharing
highlights.text: The notice states it will not disclose, sell, trade, or transfer personal data without consent, and it requires express opt-in consent before sharing data with any company outside EverAI for marketing.

changes.value: D1.1: Inputs (user prompts and outputs) are used to train EverAI's AI and moderation models on a legitimate-interest basis with no training opt-out offered

apps.name: Remini
apps.summary: Remini's GDPR-grade web policy is strong on data-subject rights, mapped legal bases, and a one-day deletion window for uploaded media. It trains on user images only when users explicitly opt in. The policy says nothing about output ownership, a published sub-processor list, or any breach-notification commitment, which holds it in the B band. A Data Protection Officer is named (Irene Pozzi, dpo@bendingspoons.com), and international transfers use named safeguards.

highlights.label: Training is opt-in only
highlights.text: User images feed generative AI training only in limited cases of "user-provided images where users have explicitly opted in to model training." By default, inputs stay out of training.

highlights.label: Fast media deletion
highlights.text: "Images, videos and audio-recordings uploaded to the web app are deleted from our servers after 1 day," and uploads from unauthenticated users are removed after 14 days.

highlights.label: Full rights with a named mechanism
highlights.text: Access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection, and consent withdrawal are all enumerated. Users exercise them in-app via My Account, then General, then Send a Privacy Request.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards, silent on breach
highlights.text: International transfers use standard contractual clauses, adequacy decisions, or binding corporate rules. The policy gives no data-breach notification commitment and publishes no sub-processor list.

apps.name: PixVerse
apps.summary: PixVerse discloses a range of data-subject rights, names standard contractual clauses as its international transfer safeguard, commits explicitly to not selling data, and permanently deletes facial data once video processing finishes. It trains on user content by default with only a conditional opt-out, and it gives no day-count or concrete deletion timeline for general data.

highlights.label: Trains by default with conditional opt-out
highlights.text: Section 1.2(12) grants the company a free, worldwide, perpetual and sublicensable licence to use Information Content in an aggregated and anonymized manner including model training and optimization, with an opt-out only where such option is made available. Training is on by default and the opt-out is conditional.

highlights.label: Facial data deleted immediately
highlights.text: For face-upload video features the policy says extracted facial data is used only for the video generation process, is not stored or shared with third parties, and is permanently deleted once processing is complete.

highlights.label: No sale of personal data
highlights.text: Section 1.3 states the company is never selling personal and sensitive user data to third parties, and defines selling as exchange or transfer for monetary compensation.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague
highlights.text: Section 4.1 commits only to retain personal data for no longer than is necessary for the stated purposes, giving no day-count and no concrete deletion timeline for general data.

changes.value: Reserves a free, worldwide, perpetual and sublicensable licence to use, reproduce, modify, distribute, and display your content (clause 1.2(12)).

apps.name: VEED
apps.summary: VEED's privacy policy is anchored to the UK GDPR and covers data-subject rights, sharing transparency, and legal-basis mapping well. By default it trains AI models on free-tier uploads under legitimate interests, and opting out means leaving the free tier. Retention is described only in general terms. The grade is capped at B by the train-by-default ceiling. No dealbreaker applies because the policy grants erasure, does not sell personal data, and offers a training opt-out.

highlights.label: Trains free-tier uploads by default
highlights.text: Section 4 describes Development and Improvement Processing that uses content uploaded under a Free subscription to train AI models on a legitimate-interests basis. Free users are in by default and can object or opt out only by leaving the free tier or deleting their account.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights with one-month response
highlights.text: Section 9 grants access, correction, erasure, objection, restriction, and portability, and commits to responding to legitimate requests within one month.

highlights.label: Does not sell personal data and names a subprocessor list
highlights.text: Section 4 states VEED does not sell personal data to third parties, and the Glossary links to a published Data Subprocessors list of service providers and sub-processors.

highlights.label: Retention stated only as long as reasonably necessary
highlights.text: Section 8 says VEED will keep personal data only for as long as reasonably necessary and tells users to request retention-period details by contacting the company. It gives no concrete day-count for general data.

apps.name: Hugging Face
apps.summary: A 2023 GDPR-based privacy policy for the platform that covers the standard mechanics: it handles data-subject rights, lists every subprocessor by name, maps legal bases, gates children under 13, and makes an explicit promise not to sell personal data. On AI-specific questions it goes quiet. It says nothing about whether user content or inputs train models, offers no training opt-out, gives no statement on who owns outputs, grants no data portability right, and uses only vague retention and deletion language with no concrete timeline. The score rewards the GDPR disclosure and penalizes the near-total silence on AI governance.

highlights.label: Does not sell personal data
highlights.text: Section 3 states the company will not sell, rent or lease personal information except as provided for in the policy, and it does not authorize collection of personally identifiable information for third-party advertising without separate member consent.

highlights.label: Named subprocessor list
highlights.text: Section 10 lists every third-party service provider in a subprocessor, description, and location table, naming AWS, Stripe, MongoDB Atlas, Google Cloud Platform, GitHub, OVHCloud, and others.

highlights.label: Silent on training and output ownership
highlights.text: The policy never says whether user content or inputs are used to train models, offers no training opt-out, and makes no statement on who owns outputs. The entire training-data domain goes unaddressed.

highlights.label: Vague retention and deletion timing
highlights.text: Retention is described only as long as necessary to deliver the services and pursue legitimate interests. Erasure is available by written request to privacy@huggingface.co with no stated completion timeline.

apps.name: Photomath
apps.summary: Photomath ships a GDPR-style policy with a wide set of data-subject rights and clear sharing rules. By default it uses your images and feedback to improve its AI, and it stays vague on retention periods, breach notice, and a training-specific opt-out. It lands at a middle grade: the rights toolkit and the do-not-sell stance are weighed down by silence on output ownership, a retention period stated only as "as long as necessary," and no named certification, breach timeline, or training opt-out.

highlights.label: Trains on your content by default
highlights.text: Section 4 says it uses the images and feedback you send to improve the service, including its math problem solving and the machine vision technologies of its affiliates and partners. Users get only a general right to object under Section 8, with no training-specific opt-out named.

highlights.label: Full rights toolkit
highlights.text: Section 8 grants access, deletion as a right to be forgotten, portability, rectification, objection, restriction, and minimization, each with a contact route. This is the strongest part of the policy.

highlights.label: Does not sell data
highlights.text: Section 6 states it does not sell, lease, or rent personal data, and Section 12 confirms for California residents that it has not sold personal information, with a Do Not Sell link offered on the main page.

highlights.label: Vague on retention and breach
highlights.text: Section 16 keeps data as long as necessary with no day-count figure, and Section 11 describes a firewalled and secured database using encryption but gives no breach notification timeline and names no certification or data protection officer.

changes.value: Uses your content to train or improve its models with no opt-out disclosed in the policy.

apps.name: Brainly
apps.summary: Brainly says twice that it does not use personal data to train models, and it grants access, deletion, portability, objection, and an advertising opt-out. It names Standard Contractual Clauses for international transfers and appoints a Data Protection Officer. Retention language is vague, with no fixed day-count and no deletion timeline, and the policy says nothing about security safeguards or breach notification. These gaps put it in the middle of band C. Brainly does share data for targeted advertising, but a named opt-out keeps that from triggering a dealbreaker.

highlights.label: No training on personal data
highlights.text: Section 9 states twice that Brainly does not use personal data to train models, and notes that Notes-feature audio recordings are not stored or processed by Brainly.

highlights.label: Complete rights set
highlights.text: Section 10 lists access, correction, deletion, machine-readable portability, objection, and restriction, plus an opt-out of sales and targeted advertising with appeal rights.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no deletion clock
highlights.text: Retention is framed as so long as it may be relevant and as long as you have an account, with no fixed day-count and no stated deletion timeline.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguard and DPO, no security section
highlights.text: Section 5 names Standard Contractual Clauses for international transfers, and the policy appoints a Data Protection Officer at privacy@brainly.com. The snapshot describes no security measures and no breach notification process.

apps.name: NotebookLM
apps.summary: NotebookLM trains foundational models only if you opt in by giving thumbs up or down feedback, and Google states it will not claim ownership over the content you generate. Through the Google Privacy Policy, you get access, deletion, export, and correction tools. The weak points: a three-year retention window for reviewed feedback, an unnamed international transfer safeguard, and no ISO or SOC 2 certification or DPO contact in the captured text. The disclosure earns a B.

highlights.label: Training is opt-in
highlights.text: The NotebookLM article states content will not be used to directly train foundational AI models unless you choose to provide feedback. Workspace and Education uploads are never used to train AI models.

highlights.label: You keep your content
highlights.text: Under Copyright, Google states it will not claim ownership over the original content you generate in NotebookLM.

highlights.label: Three-year feedback retention
highlights.text: Reviewed feedback, included content, and related data are kept for up to three years, disconnected from your Google Account. The period is named but long.

highlights.label: Strong rights tooling, weak certifications
highlights.text: The Google Privacy Policy gives you export, deletion, My Activity review, and update controls. The captured text names no ISO or SOC 2 certification and no DPO contact.

apps.name: Cohere
dealbreakerFlags: Trial-user and researcher inputs are used for research and model improvement with no opt-out; only enterprise customers can control training.
apps.summary: A full GDPR rights set with named transfer safeguards and a 30-day input retention figure, but the tier this policy governs (trial users and researchers) has its inputs and outputs used for research with no opt-out.

highlights.label: Trains on trial and research inputs with no opt-out for that tier
highlights.text: Section 2.3 says that for trial users and researchers Cohere collects the content you submit and the outputs generated and may use this input and output data to conduct research and development. Only enterprise users can control how Cohere trains on their data, so the tier this policy governs has no training opt-out. Cohere does say it takes steps to de-identify inputs and outputs before any research use.

highlights.label: 30-day input retention named for the Platform
highlights.text: Section 4 states that retention of inputs and outputs on the Platform is generally 30 days for enterprise users, a concrete day-count figure, while general personal information is kept only as long as necessary for the purposes it was collected.

highlights.label: Full rights set with self-serve account deletion
highlights.text: Users get access, correction, deletion, portability in a structured and commonly-used format, objection, and restriction. Account deletion is self-serve through the Danger Zone tab in Your Profile, and the policy honors Global Privacy Control signals for sale and share opt-out.

highlights.label: Silent on breach notification
highlights.text: Section 4 on safeguards describes administrative, technical, and physical measures plus need-to-know access, but the policy states no breach notification commitment and no timeframe anywhere in the text.

apps.name: Glean
apps.summary: A strong website and business-operations privacy policy that explicitly excludes the AI product itself, so it stays silent on training, output ownership, and AI-log retention.

highlights.label: Scope excludes the product
highlights.text: The policy states it "does not apply to your use of our products and services (collectively, 'Solutions')," so it never addresses whether copilot inputs train models or who owns outputs. Those indicators are silent rather than adverse, which is why no dealbreaker applies.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: Section 10 grants access ("Right to Know"), deletion ("Right to Delete"), correction ("Right to Correct"), portability, and objection across GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, and LGPD, all exercisable via privacy@glean.com, with a 30-day response commitment.

highlights.label: Does not sell, named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: It states "Glean does not sell, and has not sold in the preceding 12 months, Personal Information" and relies on "Standard Contractual Clauses approved by the European Commission" plus EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework certification. Both clauses appear verbatim in the snapshot.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague, no breach clause
highlights.text: Retention is described only as "as long as necessary to fulfill the legitimate business purposes," with no day-count or deletion-completion timeline. Section 6 lists generic physical, administrative, and technical safeguards but includes no breach-notification timeframe.

apps.name: Writer
apps.summary: Commits clearly never to train on your inputs or outputs and names transfer safeguards, held back by vague retention, no deletion clock, and silence on portability and breach notification.

highlights.label: Does not train on your data
highlights.text: The policy commits to improve the Services but in no event will it use any data you provide when you access or use the Services to train its models, including any inputs or outputs, which earns full credit on training-data use.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Writer certifies to the EU-U.S., UK, and Swiss Data Privacy Framework and states it may also protect personal data through standard contractual clauses, giving a concrete international transfer safeguard.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no deletion clock
highlights.text: Retention is described only as keeping information for as long as needed to fulfil the purposes it was collected for, with no day-count and no deletion timeline, so retention scores half and the deletion-timeline indicator is silent.

highlights.label: Ad-sharing with an opt-out
