highlights.text: Writer discloses device and browsing information to third-party advertising providers for targeted advertising but offers a Your privacy choices opt-out and honors Global Privacy Control, so it does not sell with no recourse.

apps.name: Synthesia
apps.summary: Strong biometric governance and a full GDPR rights set with named transfer safeguards and a DPO, held back by silence on a training opt-out, breach notification, and synthetic-output marking.

highlights.label: Biometric governance
highlights.text: The policy treats avatar Samples and Verification Recordings as biometric data under GDPR and the Illinois Biometric Privacy Act, requires explicit consent, says it permanently destroys verification biometrics once the comparison completes within a few minutes, and states it will not sell, lease, or trade biometric data.

highlights.label: Full rights and no sale
highlights.text: All GDPR rights are enumerated, covering access, erasure, rectification, objection, and portability in a structured machine-readable format, and the policy states that Synthesia does not sell or rent personal information to any third-party.

highlights.label: Vague general retention
highlights.text: Outside biometric data, Synthesia retains Other Information for as long as necessary for the purposes described, with no day-count and no fixed deletion timeline for ordinary account data.

highlights.label: Silent on breach and synthetic marking
highlights.text: The security section names no specific safeguard beyond a link to a Security Practices page, commits to no breach notification or timeframe, and the policy never addresses watermarking or labeling of AI-generated video output.

apps.name: Harvey
apps.summary: Carves user inputs and outputs out of training and processes them as a data processor, restricting training to public legal material, with full rights and named transfer safeguards but vague retention and no breach clause.

highlights.label: Inputs not used for training
highlights.text: The policy does not apply to platform input or output, which Harvey processes as a data processor under the customer agreement. AI training is restricted to publicly available material such as judgments, decisions, and public filings.

highlights.label: Full rights coverage
highlights.text: Section 10 names access, correction, deletion, portability in a standardised format, objection, restriction, and consent withdrawal. Canada and Quebec add their own access, correction, and portability rights.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: The international transfers section relies on adequacy decisions, the 4 June 2021 Standard Contractual Clauses under Article 46(2) GDPR, and certified EU-U.S., UK, and Swiss Data Privacy Framework adherence.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague
highlights.text: Retention is described only as long as necessary, with deletion in accordance with your Customer Agreement. There is no day count, no deletion-timeline number, and no breach notification clause.

apps.name: Abridge
apps.summary: Carves clinical PHI out to business associate agreements and is silent on AI model training, reserving only generic product-improvement use of non-PHI data; strong state-privacy rights but weak retention, deletion, and security specifics.

highlights.label: Training silent
highlights.text: The policy never says whether inputs are used to train AI models; the closest clause is generic, stating Abridge will conduct research and development to enhance our products, with no training opt-out named.

highlights.label: Strong rights menu
highlights.text: Section 6 grants the right to know, delete, correct, opt out of sale and sharing, and opt out of profiling, with a Do Not Sell or Share link and recognition of Global Privacy Control signals.

highlights.label: Vague retention
highlights.text: Retention is described only as long as needed to fulfill the purposes and as long as reasonably necessary, with no day-count and no deletion timeline.

highlights.label: Generic security, no breach clause
highlights.text: Section 8 commits to appropriate administrative, physical, and technical safeguards but names no specific controls like TLS or AES-256, and the policy is silent on breach notification.

apps.name: Databricks
apps.summary: An enterprise privacy notice with broad rights, named transfer safeguards, and a no-sale stance, but it gives no retention periods and does not say whether personal inputs feed model training.

highlights.label: No sale with a named opt-out
highlights.text: The notice states Databricks does not sell personal information in the traditional sense and provides a named opt-out through the Your Privacy Choices footer link, plus browser-based opt-out signals for advertising-related disclosures.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers rely on the European Commission Standard Contractual Clauses and certification under the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks, with a customer data processing addendum available.

highlights.label: Complete rights list
highlights.text: Residents can request access, a portable copy, correction, deletion, restriction or objection, and consent withdrawal through an online form or a toll-free hotline, with an appeal path if a request is denied.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague
highlights.text: Retention is described only as long as you use our Services or as required by law or for legitimate business purposes, with no day-count and no deletion timeline given.

apps.name: Snowflake
apps.summary: A thorough enterprise data-platform notice with a full rights set, named transfer safeguards, and a contactable DPO, but it says nothing about model training and gives only vague retention.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: Section 14 grants access, correction, deletion, portability, objection, restriction, and consent withdrawal. Users exercise them through named OneTrust webforms, a phone line at 1-877-243-8061, and recognition of the Global Privacy Control signal.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 10 relies on standard contractual clauses and self-certifies under the EU-U.S., UK extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks, with a linked sub-processors and affiliates list.

highlights.label: Vague retention only
highlights.text: Section 16 keeps personal information for as long as there is an ongoing legitimate business need and commits to delete or anonymize it afterward, with no stated number of days, so retention scores low.

highlights.label: Shares for ad targeting with opt-out
highlights.text: Snowflake states it does not sell personal information. The California table discloses sharing Identifiers and Internet Activity for cross-context behavioral advertising, and offers an opt-out through cookie settings, the Global Privacy Control, and a privacy webform.

apps.name: Granola
apps.summary: Says customer personal data is never used to train its models (only de-identified data is, with an opt-out), grants the full GDPR rights, and keeps meeting recordings at zero retention.

highlights.label: Personal data not used for training
highlights.text: The policy states "We do not allow third parties such as OpenAI or Anthropic to use your Personal Data to train AI models" and uses only de-identified data for training, which users can opt out of within their Granola account settings.

highlights.label: Recordings kept at zero retention
highlights.text: Recordings are captured only to produce a transcription: "We do not retain or store such recordings once the transcription is created," so nothing from the capture step is kept.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights with named channels
highlights.text: Access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection, restriction, and withdrawal of consent are all granted, exercised by emailing privacy@granola.so or through in-app account settings.

highlights.label: Does not sell and names a transfer safeguard
highlights.text: The policy says "we do not currently sell your Personal Data," references a published Subprocessors list, and relies on "standard data protection clauses" for U.S. transfers.

apps.name: Salesforce Einstein
apps.summary: Salesforce Einstein sits at the top of the B band because it guarantees zero data retention for AI prompts, states clearly that the customer owns the data, and lists every data-subject right. It misses an A because controller-side retention timing is vague, the security language is generic, and the policy is silent on breach notification and government access standards.

highlights.label: AI prompts never stored or trained on
highlights.text: The Trust Layer states that zero data retention is a strict policy: prompts and generated responses are never stored or used to train the underlying third-party large language models, and PII is masked before the prompt reaches the model.

highlights.label: Customer owns the data and outputs
highlights.text: Salesforce commits that the data it manages does not belong to Salesforce, it belongs to the customer. Permission-aware dynamic grounding then limits the model to customer-approved sources.

highlights.label: Full rights and named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 10 grants access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, and objection. Section 7 names SCCs under Article 46 plus EU-US, Swiss-US, and UK DPF certification for international transfers.

highlights.label: Weak spots on retention timing and breach notice
highlights.text: Controller-side retention is described only as 'as long as required' with deletion after applicable retention periods and no number, security is described in generic terms, and the policy says nothing about breach notification or any government access standard.

apps.name: GitHub Copilot
dealbreakerFlags: The governing GitHub General Privacy Statement processes user content, including code, inputs, and AI outputs, to train AI and machine-learning models under legitimate interests, applying de-identification only where feasible, with no named consumer training opt-out in the captured text.
apps.summary: GitHub Copilot runs under a detailed Microsoft and GitHub privacy statement that meets GDPR standards. It gives users the full set of data-subject rights, names standard contractual clauses and Data Privacy Framework safeguards for international transfers, and states data categories and legal bases clearly. The same statement says it uses user content, including code, inputs, and AI outputs, to train AI and machine learning models under legitimate interests. De-identification applies only where feasible, the captured text names no consumer training opt-out, and retention is described only in vague terms with no day count or deletion timeline.

highlights.label: Trains on user content, opt-out not named
highlights.text: The statement uses Personal Data, including code, inputs, and AI outputs, to develop and improve artificial intelligence and machine learning technologies, including training models, under legitimate interests. Aggregation and de-identification apply only where feasible, and no consumer mechanism is named to exclude inputs from training.

highlights.label: Full set of data-subject rights
highlights.text: Users can access, rectify, erase, restrict, object, withdraw consent, and request portability in a machine-readable format by emailing privacy at github. The statement names a DPO and a right to complain to a Data Protection Authority.

highlights.label: Strong international transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Transfers from the EU, UK, and Switzerland rely on the European Commission standard contractual clauses under Decision 2021/914, and GitHub certifies to the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Framework under FTC enforcement.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague
highlights.text: Data is kept as long as the account is active and as needed for contractual, legal, and dispute purposes. US-state text mentions brief retention terms by design, and the governing statement gives no day count or deletion timeline.

apps.name: Ambience Healthcare
apps.summary: Ambience Healthcare's privacy policy grants consumer and GDPR data-subject rights: access, deletion, correction, portability, and opt-out. It does not say whether user inputs train AI models, gives no day-count retention period or deletion timeline, makes no breach-notification commitment, and claims no security certification. Clinical and patient data are scoped out to HIPAA business associate agreements with its provider customers, so the consumer-facing policy never addresses the most safety-relevant training and retention disclosures.

highlights.label: Full rights catalog
highlights.text: The policy grants access, deletion, correction, portability, and opt-out rights to residents of applicable states and to EEA, Switzerland, and UK individuals, including the right to delete personal information that we have obtained and the right to receive data in a machine-readable format.

highlights.label: Silent on AI training
highlights.text: Ambience is a clinical documentation AI vendor, yet the policy never says whether user inputs train models and names no training opt-out. It scopes clinical data to HIPAA business associate agreements with provider customers.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no breach clause
highlights.text: Retention is described only as not retaining information for longer than needed to fulfill these purposes, with no day-count and no deletion timeline. The policy makes no breach-notification commitment.

highlights.label: Ad sharing with opt-out
highlights.text: The policy discloses sharing email addresses with Facebook Custom Audiences and lists categories that may be sold or shared in its California notice. It also grants a right to opt out of the sale or sharing of personal information.

apps.name: OpenHands
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: content and feedback used to train and tune AI models with no opt-out mechanism disclosed
apps.summary: All Hands AI's policy is a general data-handling document that says it uses user content and feedback to train and tune its AI models, with no opt-out disclosed, and it says little about most data-subject rights, retention periods, or security specifics. It lists sharing categories, confirms it does not use submitted content for advertising, and includes a children age gate, but the training-with-no-opt-out reservation is an adverse term that caps the displayed grade.

highlights.label: Trains on content, no opt-out
highlights.text: The policy says it may use content and feedback from your use of the Services to train and tune its AI models, and it names no opt-out mechanism for that training anywhere in the document.

highlights.label: Does not use submitted content for ads
highlights.text: On interest-based advertising the policy is explicit that it does not use any content you submit for these advertising purposes. That is a clear limit, even though it runs cookie-based ads through third-party partners.

highlights.label: Vague retention, deletion by email
highlights.text: Retention is described only as long as reasonably necessary, with no day count, and deletion is offered by emailing contact@all-hands.dev with no completion timeline given.

highlights.label: Thin rights and generic security
highlights.text: Beyond content deletion and a marketing unsubscribe, the policy says nothing about access, portability, or rectification, and security is described only as technical, organizational and physical safeguards with no named controls and no breach notification.

apps.name: Fyxer
apps.summary: Fyxer's policy bars its AI providers from training on user data, requires explicit in-app consent before that data is shared, and has a zero data retention agreement with its AI service provider. It also names international transfer safeguards. The retention language stays vague, it gives no deletion timeline or shorter AI-log retention, and it says nothing about output ownership or security certifications, because the terms of service that would govern ownership are not part of this privacy policy snapshot.

highlights.label: No AI training on your data
highlights.text: Section 5.1 requires AI providers to use information only to deliver the service and states that the policy does not allow those providers to train their AI models on user data. This earns full credit on the training-data indicator.

highlights.label: Consent gate before AI sharing
highlights.text: Section 5.2 says Fyxer seeks explicit user consent before sharing data with its AI models, shown as an in-app prompt at setup or when the policy changes significantly.

highlights.label: Zero data retention with the AI provider
highlights.text: Section 5.3 names a Zero Data Retention agreement under which the AI service provider does not store customer API data on its servers. This is a concrete limit on sharing.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 7 relies on a contract in a form approved by the European Commission and on EU-US Data Privacy Framework membership for US providers, both named mechanisms for international transfers.

apps.name: Sierra
apps.summary: Sierra's corporate privacy policy covers a full set of data-subject rights, names its international transfer safeguards, and maps its legal bases clearly. It does not say whether site or demo-agent data is used to train Sierra models, gives no retention day-count, and makes no breach-notification commitment. The combination of solid rights handling and these core gaps puts it at the top of the C band. The policy explicitly excludes customer data processed through Sierra's AI agents, which separate customer contracts and DPAs govern.

highlights.label: Full rights suite
highlights.text: The policy grants access, portability, deletion, correction, and the right to opt out or object. Data portability is described as an electronic copy in a structured, commonly used, and machine readable format.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers rely on Standard Contractual Clauses, including the EU Standard Contractual Clauses, and Sierra is certified to the EU-US, Swiss-US, and UK Extension to the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.

highlights.label: Silent on model training
highlights.text: The policy mentions generative AI service providers that aggregate and analyze data, but it never states whether personal information from the site or demo agents trains Sierra models, and it offers no training opt-out.

highlights.label: Vague retention and no breach clause
highlights.text: Retention is stated only as long as you use our Services or as necessary to fulfill the purposes, with no day-count, and the policy contains no breach notification commitment.

apps.name: Decagon
apps.summary: Decagon's published policy covers only its own website and marketing data. It says nothing about AI training, retention periods, or most data-subject rights, because the data its AI agents process is excluded and left to private customer agreements.

highlights.label: Training use unaddressed
highlights.text: The policy states it does not apply to information processed on behalf of business customers, which separate customer agreements govern. It never says whether inputs are used to train models, so the training, opt-out, and tier indicators all score zero for silence.

highlights.label: No retention disclosure
highlights.text: The policy gives no retention period, no deletion timeline, and no shorter AI-log retention statement. The retention domain stays at zero, with only partial credit for the de-identification and aggregation language.

highlights.label: Sharing categories are clear
highlights.text: The policy lists recipient categories: service providers, advertising partners, professional advisors, authorities, and business transferees. It also names opt-out tools for interest-based advertising through the Network Advertising Initiative, the Digital Advertising Alliance, and platform settings.

highlights.label: Rights are only a generic gesture
highlights.text: Beyond a marketing unsubscribe option and a children-specific deletion path, the policy only invites users to contact it to exercise any privacy-related right that may be available under applicable law. It names no access, deletion, portability, or rectification mechanism.

apps.name: Qwen
apps.summary: Scored on Alibaba Cloud's generic policy, which grants core rights and names transfer safeguards but omits any AI-specific disclosure.

highlights.label: Rights
highlights.text: Access, deletion, correction and portability, with stronger EEA/UK object and restriction rights.

highlights.label: Transfers
highlights.text: Names the EU-US Data Privacy Framework and Standard Contractual Clauses.

highlights.label: Training
highlights.text: Entirely silent on whether user inputs train models, with no opt-in or opt-out.

highlights.label: AI transparency
highlights.text: Does not disclose that Qwen is an AI service or mark synthetic output; reads as generic cloud infrastructure.

apps.name: Poe
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: developers who build bots and apps on Poe may view and store chats on their servers to train their models, with no opt-out mechanism disclosed in this policy
apps.summary: Poe's privacy policy ties a legal basis to each collection purpose and lists its third-party recipients. It gives no retention periods, deletion timelines, security safeguards, or breach notification, and it sends all data-subject rights to a separate Quora Privacy Policy that is not included in this document. The policy also says that bot and app developers using Poe's APIs may store user chats on their servers to train their models, with no opt-out named here. That meets the training dealbreaker.

highlights.label: Chats fuel third-party training
highlights.text: The policy says developers who build bots and apps on Poe using APIs may view and store your chats on their servers to train their models, and that AI model providers receive chat contents and uploaded files to improve their services. No opt-out mechanism is named in this document.

highlights.label: Rights deferred to another policy
highlights.text: For access, deletion, portability, and other rights, the policy points users to the separate Quora Privacy Policy and names no specific mechanism here.

highlights.label: Silent on retention and security
highlights.text: The snapshot gives no retention period, no deletion timeline, no description of security safeguards, and no breach notification commitment.

highlights.label: Third parties and legal bases are mapped
highlights.text: Each collection section names a legal basis such as contractual obligation, legitimate interest, or consent. The policy lists billing, tax, advertising, payment, and AI model provider recipients, and it names a Data Protection Officer reachable at privacy@quora.com.

changes.value: D1.1: developers who build bots and apps on Poe may view and store chats on their servers to train their models, with no opt-out mechanism disclosed in this policy
Third-party developers may train models on user chats, with no opt-in or opt-out disclosed.
Hashed email is shared with ad-platform partners for advertising, with no do-not-sell provision.

apps.name: CrushOn AI
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: User Content from character chats is used to train AI models with no opt-out offered
apps.summary: CrushOn AI trains its models on user chat content by default and offers no training opt-out anywhere in its rights and choices section, which is an explicit adverse reservation. It also gives only contact-only data rights, states a vague "as long as reasonably necessary" retention with no day count, and sets no concrete deletion timeline. The score of 45 falls below the ChatGPT calibration anchor of 76 (which has an opt-out and a 30-day deletion window), and the missing opt-out is the reason for the gap.

highlights.label: Trains on chats, no opt-out
highlights.text: The policy says it may use User Content from character chats to train AI models and lists no training opt-out among user choices. This raises the training dealbreaker flag and caps the displayed grade at B.

highlights.label: Retention stays vague
highlights.text: Retention is described only as for so long as it is reasonably necessary or required by law, with no day count and no concrete deletion timeline.

highlights.label: Generic security, no certifications
highlights.text: Security is limited to commercially reasonable security measures, with no named TLS, AES-256, or RBAC and no SOC 2 or ISO certification claimed. A Data Protection Officer contact is provided.

highlights.label: Clear age gate and AI disclosure
highlights.text: The policy requires users to be at least 18, commits to deleting under-18 data where required by law, and openly describes the service as AI chats with characters powered by AI models.

changes.value: D1.1: User Content from character chats is used to train AI models with no opt-out offered

apps.name: Cursor
apps.summary: Cursor earns a strong C. The policy sets a default no-training stance for Inputs and Suggestions, the strongest posture on training data, and Section 6 grants a full set of data-subject rights with a contact address and an appeal path. The grade is lowered by vague retention language, no mention of breach notification, and no claimed certifications. The dealbreaker list is correctly empty, because the policy reserves no harmful right. Output ownership is not covered here because the Cursor Terms of Service fell outside the captured snapshot.

highlights.label: Training is opt-in by default
highlights.text: Anysphere says it does not use Inputs or Suggestions to train its models, and does not let third parties do so, unless content is flagged for security review, explicitly reported, or the user has explicitly agreed. This is the strongest training-data posture in the rubric.

highlights.label: Full set of user rights
highlights.text: Section 6 lists access, portability, deletion, correction, objection, restriction, and consent withdrawal, all exercisable by emailing hi@cursor.com, with an appeal path if a request is denied.

highlights.label: No sale or targeted advertising
highlights.text: The policy says it does not sell or share personal data for cross-contextual behavioral advertising and does not process personal data for targeted advertising as defined under US state privacy laws.

highlights.label: Retention and security stay vague
highlights.text: Retention is described only as long as necessary, with no day-count and no deletion timeline. Security is described as commercially reasonable measures with no named controls, and breach notification is absent.

apps.name: Lovable
apps.summary: Lovable's privacy policy covers more than most developer tools. It says raw or identifiable personal data is not used for training, gives a named opt-out, and confirms that customers own their Customer Data and AI Output. It lists the full set of data-subject rights with a 30-day response window, names the safeguards for international transfers, and sets out specific security and breach-notification controls. The retention period is the only item below a full award: it is capped at 90 days, which falls in the middle band.

highlights.label: Training opt-out and output ownership
highlights.text: Raw or identifiable personal data is not used for training. Customers can opt out by emailing privacy@lovable.dev or upgrading to a Business plan, and the policy confirms that you own Customer Data and AI Output while Lovable owns Usage Data.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: The policy lists access, portability, deletion, correction, withdrawal of consent, and opt-out of sale or sharing, all exercisable by email with a 30-day response window.

highlights.label: Named transfer and security controls
highlights.text: International transfers rely on EU SCCs Module 2, the UK IDTA version B1.0, and a Swiss addendum. Security covers role-based access, multi-factor authentication, encryption, annual SOC 2 Type II audits, ISO 27001-certified data centers, and a 72-hour breach notification commitment.

highlights.label: Retention capped at 90 days
highlights.text: Customer data and log data are retained for up to ninety days, with personal data deleted within 30 days of account termination and backups held up to 90 days. This retention anchor falls in the middle band.

apps.name: PolyBuzz
apps.summary: PolyBuzz publishes a detailed, region-specific privacy policy with broad data-subject rights and an explicit no-sale stance. It also trains its AI on user-uploaded photos and precise geolocation by default. The only control is a consent permission you can withdraw in Settings, and there is no separate training opt-out. The policy gives no output-ownership terms and no named retention period. Training by default plus a vague retention commitment puts it in the B band.

highlights.label: Trains on user content by default
highlights.text: The User Consent section grants permission to collect photos you upload and to use precise geolocation to train our AI algorithm, so uploads feed model training. The only control is withdrawing consent in Settings, which disables the feature. There is no separate training opt-out.

highlights.label: Does not sell data
highlights.text: Section 6.3 states PolyBuzz DO NOT sell User Information to any other individual, company, or organization, points users to the DPO if they suspect a sale, and provides a named Opt-out Request mechanism for ad sharing.

highlights.label: Clear deletion and rights
highlights.text: Section 7.3 gives a named deletion path through Setting, Account, Delete. Sections 7.1 to 7.4 cover access, correction, portability, and a 30-day response window for rights requests.

highlights.label: Vague retention and generic security
highlights.text: Section 8.1 commits only to retain data for no longer than is necessary, with no day-count. Section 9.1 relies on industry-standard technical security measures and names no TLS, encryption standard, or certification.

apps.name: Genspark
apps.summary: Genspark earns a B by combining a no-sell commitment and a no-train posture toward user inputs with a full set of data-subject rights and a stated 30-day account-deletion timeline. The grade is lower because the policy says nothing about breach notification and describes security only in generic terms, with no named encryption or access controls. The policy is the correct entity (MainFunc/Genspark Inc., genspark.ai) and resolves cleanly against quoted clauses, so confidence is high. The policy reserves no dealbreaker rights: it does not claim to train on prompts, take a content licence, or refuse deletion, and it explicitly does not sell.

highlights.label: Does not train on your inputs
highlights.text: The policy routes prompts to third-party providers such as OpenAI and Anthropic only to generate responses, says it is not their practice to collect or analyze that data, and states that Google Workspace data is not used to develop, improve, or train generalized AI or ML models.

highlights.label: Does not sell or share for ads
highlights.text: Genspark says it does not sell, trade, or share personal data, confirms it does not use data for targeted advertising, and honors Universal Opt-out Mechanism browser signals.

highlights.label: Deletion within 30 days
highlights.text: If you close your account, the policy commits to deleting account data from servers within 30 days, and it names access, deletion, portability, correction, and objection rights.

highlights.label: Breach notification is silent
highlights.text: The Security of Information section promises reasonable safeguards but says nothing about notifying users of a data breach, and it describes security only in generic terms, with no named TLS or encryption controls.

apps.name: Suno
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: Suno trains its models on user content (Submissions, Interactive Chat Information, and other Content) by default with no opt-out offered anywhere in the notice
apps.summary: Suno discloses data-subject rights and promises that it does not sell or share personal information. It also reserves the right to train its models on user content by default, and the notice offers no opt-out anywhere, which is what caps its trust ceiling. Retention and deletion are described only in vague terms. The one concrete number is a three-year cap on biometric voice data.

highlights.label: Trains on user content by default
highlights.text: The notice states that Suno uses Submissions, Interactive Chat Information, and other Content to train and improve the models that power its Services. It offers no opt-out for that training.

highlights.label: Does not sell data
highlights.text: Suno states that it does not sell your information to third parties. The U.S. residents section confirms it does not sell or share personal information as defined under the CCPA, and has not done so in the preceding 12 months.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: The notice grants access, deletion, portability, correction, and objection rights. The EEA and California sections name the right to a copy, transfer in a readable format, correction, and deletion. You exercise all of them by emailing privacy@suno.com.

highlights.label: Retention stated only in vague terms
highlights.text: Suno says it retains information for as long as is reasonably necessary and will action deletion within a reasonable period of time. The one concrete figure is a three-year cap on biometric voice data.

changes.value: D1.1: Suno trains its models on user content (Submissions, Interactive Chat Information, and other Content) by default with no opt-out offered anywhere in the notice

apps.name: CapCut
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: the policy affirmatively states it uses user content to train, test, and improve machine learning models and maps this to legitimate interests, with no opt-out mechanism named anywhere, so inputs are used to train technology by default
apps.summary: CapCut earns strong marks for data-subject rights, legal-basis mapping, and international transfer safeguards, but it trains technology on user content with no opt-out and stays vague on retention, security specifics, and sub-processors. The training-by-default language in the How We Use section and the EEA legal-basis table is an explicit reservation, so the D1.1 dealbreaker stands. The policy has no fabricated certifications, the output-ownership indicator is correctly treated as silent because no Terms of Service text is present, and the score lands in the C band.

highlights.label: Trains by default, no opt-out
highlights.text: Section 2 lists using information to train, test, and improve technology such as machine learning models and algorithms, and the EEA legal-basis table maps this to legitimate interests. No opt-out mechanism is named anywhere in the policy.

highlights.label: Full rights suite in EU terms
highlights.text: The European Region section grants access to a copy, rectification or erasure, data portability in a structured machine-readable format, objection, and restriction. This covers all five data-subject rights with concrete mechanisms.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: The international transfers section relies on Article 45 adequacy decisions and Article 46 standard contractual clauses for transfers to countries without an adequacy decision, which satisfies the named-safeguard test.

highlights.label: Retention left vague
highlights.text: Section 7 states only that CapCut retains information for as long as necessary to provide the Services. There is no general deletion timeline in the main body, so it scores a half on the retention-period ladder.

changes.value: D1.1: the policy affirmatively states it uses user content to train, test, and improve machine learning models and maps this to legitimate interests, with no opt-out mechanism named anywhere, so inputs are used to train technology by default

apps.name: SpicyChat
apps.summary: SpicyChat uses a generic May 2023 template privacy policy that grants basic access, deletion, and rectification rights but leaves out the details an AI chat app needs. It never says whether chat inputs train or fine-tune models, gives no training opt-out, names no retention period, and covers neither breach notification nor any security certification. The policy is the genuine SpicyChat.AI document operated by NextDay AI. The score reflects real coverage of basic rights, lowered by broad gaps in disclosure, with no explicit harmful reservation in the text.

highlights.label: Silent on training
highlights.text: The policy never says whether chat inputs are used to train or fine-tune AI models. The only related language is the generic "evaluate and improve our Service," so there is no training disclosure and no opt-out mechanism.

highlights.label: Deletion right granted
highlights.text: "You have the right to delete or request that We assist in deleting the Personal Data that We have collected about You," backed by self-service account deletion and a contact route, though no deletion timeline is given.

highlights.label: Vague retention only
highlights.text: Retention is described as "only for as long as is necessary for the purposes set out," with usage data "generally retained for a shorter period." No day-count or concrete deletion timeframe appears anywhere.

highlights.label: Generic security, no breach notice
highlights.text: Security is limited to "commercially acceptable means" with no named controls such as TLS or AES-256, and the policy is silent on breach notification, a data protection officer, and any certifications.

apps.name: Otter.ai
dealbreakerFlags: D1.1: Section 2 lists a processing purpose of "training our proprietary AI technology on de-identified audio recordings and on transcriptions (which may contain Personal Information)" with no training opt-out mechanism named anywhere in the policy, so training on user content is on by default.
apps.summary: Otter.ai lists data-subject rights, names its subprocessors, and covers international transfers with the EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework certification and Standard Contractual Clauses. Training and retention are the weak points. The policy says Otter trains its own AI on meeting audio and transcriptions that may contain personal information, and it names no opt-out, so training is on by default. Retention is described only as "as long as necessary," with no day-count and no concrete deletion timeline. The active training dealbreaker caps the displayed grade at B, but the score of 65 already lands in the C band.

highlights.label: Trains on your meetings
highlights.text: Section 2 lists a purpose of "training our proprietary AI technology on de-identified audio recordings and on transcriptions (which may contain Personal Information)" and names no opt-out, so training on user content is on by default.

highlights.label: Strong rights menu
highlights.text: Section 6 grants access, correction, erasure, portability in a machine-readable format, objection, restriction, and consent withdrawal, with a privacy@otter.ai contact and a toll-free opt-out line.

highlights.label: Sells data but offers opt-out
highlights.text: The policy says some disclosures to advertising and analytics providers "may broadly be considered a sale of Personal Information" and points users to a "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" link in the website footer.

highlights.label: Retention left vague
highlights.text: Section 5 says Otter stores all personal information "for as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes set out in this Policy," with no day-count and no concrete deletion timeline.

apps.name: Gong
apps.summary: Gong's privacy policy is strong on data-subject rights, international transfer safeguards, and accountability. It gives no retention periods, names no training opt-out, and makes no breach notification commitment, so the overall grade is a C.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: Section 9 grants access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, and portability across GDPR, UK GDPR, LGPD, CCPA and FADP, and names Prighter as the privacy representative for EU and UK requests.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 3 names EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Framework certification, an Israel adequacy basis, and Standard Contractual Clauses for transfers to countries without an adequacy decision.

highlights.label: Retention left vague
highlights.text: Section 4 keeps personal data for as long as Gong deems reasonably necessary under its data retention policy. It gives no day count and no deletion timeline.

highlights.label: No training opt-out and no breach clause
highlights.text: Section 2 uses data to optimize AI and machine learning capabilities under legitimate interests with no opt-out named, and Section 8 describes security in general terms with no breach notification commitment.

apps.name: Ironclad
apps.summary: Ironclad's privacy policy gives clear data-subject rights and names specific international-transfer safeguards. It says nothing about AI model training or about input and output ownership, and it shares identifiers for interest-based advertising under a named opt-out. Good rights and transfer disclosure alongside no mention of training or content use place the policy in the middle of the C band.

highlights.label: Sells or shares for ads with opt-out
highlights.text: The policy states that disclosing identifiers, device identifiers and cookies for interest-based advertising may be a sale or sharing under the CCPA. It offers a named opt-out through the Your Privacy Choices link, a privacy email, and the Global Privacy Control signal.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers rely on signed Standard Contractual Clauses, and Ironclad states it is certified under the Data Privacy Framework. Both appear explicitly in the International Data Transfers section.

highlights.label: Silent on AI training
highlights.text: The policy never addresses whether user-submitted contract content or other inputs are used to train AI or machine-learning models. The training-use indicators score zero because of this silence, with no harmful reservation stated either way.

highlights.label: Vague retention only
highlights.text: Retention is described as no longer than reasonably necessary to fulfil the purposes for which it was collected, with no day count and no deletion timeline. It scores as a vague half, short of a concrete period.

apps.name: Sana
apps.summary: Sana's privacy notice is a GDPR-grade document. It states twice that Sana and its third-party service providers do not use personal data to train artificial intelligence or machine learning models, and it commits that Sana will not sell personal data. Three areas are weak. Core service retention is described only as account lifetime or as long as necessary. Output IP ownership is not addressed, because no terms were captured. Security controls are described generically, with no named TLS or AES-256.

highlights.label: No model training
highlights.text: The notice states twice that Sana and any of its third-party service providers do not use your personal data to train artificial intelligence or machine learning models, with Civic Tier and Company Subscriber content excluded from any content review.

highlights.label: Does not sell data
highlights.text: Sana commits that it will not sell your personal data, and confirms under the CCPA that it does not sell or share personal information as those terms are defined under the CCPA and CPRA.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: Access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, consent withdrawal, and a structured machine-readable portability right are each spelled out, with a one-month response deadline.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguard
highlights.text: Processing happens within the EU and EEA. Transfers to third countries rely on adequacy, the European Commission's standard contractual clauses, explicit consent, or article 49 GDPR derogations.

apps.name: Luminance
apps.summary: Luminance publishes a GDPR-shaped website privacy policy with broad data-subject rights and named cross-border transfer safeguards. It says it will not sell or rent data and does not share data for marketing. The policy is silent on retention periods and deletion timelines, gives no AI-interaction or product-input disclosure (it is scoped to the website), and reserves the right to change the policy at any time without notice.

highlights.label: Full GDPR rights
highlights.text: The policy lists access, correction, erasure, objection, restriction, portability, and consent withdrawal, and states a one-month response window under Time Limit to Respond.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: It relies on standard data protection clauses approved by the European Commission and certifies to the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks for transfers to the United States.

highlights.label: No sale or marketing sharing
highlights.text: The Marketing section says Luminance will not sell or rent your data to third parties or share it with third parties for marketing purposes.

highlights.label: Silent on retention, changeable without notice
highlights.text: No retention period or deletion timeline is given for personal data, and the policy reserves the right to amend at any time, for any reason, without notice to you.

apps.name: Tabnine
apps.summary: Tabnine earns a solid GDPR-style data-subject rights section and an explicit no-sale promise, but its privacy policy stays completely silent on whether user code and inputs train its AI models, and it gives only vague retention and security commitments. For a coding assistant the missing training disclosure is a meaningful gap, and the score reflects strong rights handling alongside that silence. The grade survives adversarial review: no dealbreaker was raised on silence, the no-sell clause is favorable rather than adverse, no certification or mechanism was fabricated, and the score and letter sit correctly below the ChatGPT calibration anchor.

highlights.label: Training silence
highlights.text: For an AI coding assistant the policy never says whether user code or inputs are used to train models, and no opt-out is named anywhere, so the whole training-data domain scores zero on documented silence.

highlights.label: Strong rights
highlights.text: The Your Rights section grants access, rectification, erasure, portability, and objection, and the California section adds a verified request process with a 45-day response window that can extend to 90 days.

highlights.label: Does not sell
highlights.text: The California notice states plainly that Tabnine does not sell your Personal Information and does not share sensitive Personal Information for cross-context behavioral advertising.

highlights.label: Vague retention and security
highlights.text: Retention is described only as not retaining information longer than necessary with no day count, and security is described as industry standard measures, with a breach response that promises to minimize harm but names no notification timeframe.

apps.name: Replit
apps.summary: Replit discloses data-subject rights across six US state regimes plus the EEA and UK, and commits to a 30-day account deletion window. It relies on a legitimate interest to improve the accuracy of its machine learning technologies such as code generation, names no training opt-out, and says nothing about output ownership or specific security controls, which holds the score in the low C band.

highlights.label: Trains on code with no opt-out
highlights.text: The policy claims a legitimate interest in using information to improve the accuracy of its machine learning technologies such as code generation, and names no way to opt out of that use.

highlights.label: Clear deletion timeline
highlights.text: When you request to delete your account, Replit deletes your data within 30 days. The policy gives a specific number.

highlights.label: Does not sell personal information
highlights.text: Across the California, Colorado, Connecticut, Iowa, Utah, and Virginia sections, the policy repeats that it does not sell personal information as that term is defined under each state law.

highlights.label: Broad data-subject rights with a named channel
highlights.text: EEA and UK users get access, correction, restriction, objection, anonymization or deletion, and portability. State residents submit access and deletion requests by emailing privacy@replit.com or through account settings.

apps.name: Sourcegraph Cody
apps.summary: Sourcegraph's controller-level privacy policy is strong on data-subject rights, transparency, and sharing limits, and it commits not to extract, store, or use personal data from user code for any purpose. It loses points for vague retention language, no named security controls or certifications, and no breach-notification timeline.

highlights.label: Code is not used for training
highlights.text: The policy says Sourcegraph does not intentionally extract, store, or use personal data from User Content for any purpose, that analytics and usage data exclude code, and that work product is owned by your organization.

highlights.label: Full rights with a defined response window
highlights.text: Access, portability, correction, deletion, restriction, and objection are all granted, and verified requests are answered within one month, with a possible extension of up to two further months.

highlights.label: No sale and a named transfer safeguard
highlights.text: The policy states Sourcegraph does not sell or share personal data for cross-context behavioral advertising and relies on Standard Contractual Clauses approved by the European Commission and the UK ICO for transfers to the United States.

highlights.label: Vague retention and thin security detail
highlights.text: Retention is described only as as long as reasonably necessary, security is framed as appropriate technical and organizational measures with no named controls or certifications, and there is no breach-notification clause.

apps.name: Windsurf
apps.summary: Windsurf runs on the Cognition AI privacy policy, which names Windsurf as a covered Service. The policy lists a full set of data-subject rights and states that it does not sell or share personal information for targeted advertising. It names Standard Contractual Clauses and the UK International Data Transfer Addendum for international transfers. Three areas are weak: the model-training opt-out is conditional with no named mechanism, retention is given only as as needed with no timeline, and security rests on commercially reasonable measures with no named controls, breach notice, or certification. The policy scores at the top of band C.

highlights.label: Training opt-out unclear
highlights.text: Section 2 says it may use User Content to train, fine-tune and improve its models depending on the terms that apply to your use of the Services, and the policy names no opt-out mechanism.

highlights.label: Full rights suite
highlights.text: Section 8 grants access, portability in a structured machine-readable format, deletion, rectification, objection, consent withdrawal, and appeal, all exercisable at privacy@cognition.ai.

highlights.label: Does not sell or share for ads
highlights.text: Section 3 states it does not sell or share personal information for targeted advertising and has not done so in the past 12 months.

highlights.label: Vague retention and generic security
highlights.text: Section 5 keeps data as needed with no stated timeline, and Section 4 relies on commercially reasonable measures with no named controls, breach-notice commitment, or certification.

apps.name: Clay
apps.summary: Clay's privacy policy is strong on data-subject rights, sharing transparency, and international transfer safeguards. It says nothing about AI model training and gives little detail on retention or breach notification, which keeps the score mid-table.

highlights.label: Self-declared data broker with sell opt-out
highlights.text: Section 13 states "Yes. Clay is a data broker" and offers a "do not sell" button plus a privacy@clay.com route. The 2024 metrics show all 8 opt-out requests were honored. The sale of data is disclosed and an opt-out exists, so it does not sink the score.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights toolkit
highlights.text: Sections 11 and 12 grant access, correction, portability, deletion, objection, and restriction across the European and U.S. state frameworks, with a privacy@clay.com request channel and published CCPA response metrics.

highlights.label: International transfers with named safeguards
highlights.text: Section 14 relies on European Commission adequacy decisions, contractual protections, and Binding Corporate Rules, and Section 17 appoints VeraSafe as the Article 27 EU representative.

highlights.label: Retention and breach handling underspecified
highlights.text: Section 5 keeps data "only as long as necessary" with no day-count, and Section 6 promises "appropriate technical and organizational security measures" with no breach-notification commitment or timeframe. AI model training is not addressed at all.

apps.name: Apollo.io
apps.summary: Apollo.io discloses strong data-subject rights, named international transfer safeguards, and a clear AI opt-out. Its data-broker contributory database, vague retention with no day count, and silence on output ownership and breach notification keep it at a mid C. Selling and sharing personal data comes with a named opt-out (the California sale or sharing right plus the database removal request), which keeps it a half-credit weakness.

highlights.label: AI training opt-out is named
highlights.text: The policy states Apollo may use Service Information and business contact data as inputs to internally developed models and third-party AI providers, and it grants data subjects the right to object or opt out through the Privacy Center and Remove Center.

highlights.label: Data-broker reuse is broad
highlights.text: Data submitted to Apollo is used to grow, enrich, and verify its Contributory Database, which is made available to all other Apollo customers, and Apollo is registered as a data broker in California and other states.

highlights.label: Transfer safeguards are named
highlights.text: Apollo certifies to the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks and relies on Standard Contractual Clauses with Transfer Impact Assessments for transfers outside the EEA.

highlights.label: Retention and breach notice are weak
highlights.text: Retention is described only as for as long as it is useful in our products, with no day count, and the security section asks users to report breaches without committing the provider to any breach-notification timeframe.

apps.name: Copy.ai
apps.summary: Copy.ai discloses strong GDPR and US-state data-subject rights, names EU-US Data Privacy Framework and standard contractual clause transfer safeguards, and states it does not sell personal data or share it for cross-contextual advertising. It stays silent on whether user prompts and uploaded Content train its AI models, and it gives only a vague "as long as necessary" retention period with no day counts or deletion timeline.

highlights.label: Training silent
highlights.text: The purposes section lists developing new products and improving the Services, but the notice never says whether prompts, uploaded files, or Content are used to train AI models. No opt-out is offered.

highlights.label: Strong rights
highlights.text: Section 10 grants access, rectification, deletion, portability, objection, and consent withdrawal for EEA, UK and Swiss residents. Section 11 adds access, correction, deletion, and opt-out rights for US state residents through legal@copy.ai.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Sections 7 and 8 cite the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, the UK Extension, the Swiss-US DPF, and European Commission standard contractual clauses, with FTC oversight and JAMS as the dispute-resolution provider.

highlights.label: Vague retention and security
highlights.text: Section 13 keeps data for different periods and as long as you use the Services, with no day count or deletion timeline. Section 6 describes safeguards only as industry standard security, with no breach notification commitment.

apps.name: Vanta
apps.summary: Vanta's controller-level website privacy policy earns a mid-C. It has a complete data-subject rights menu, a named subprocessor disclosure, and named international transfer safeguards. It says nothing about AI training and gives only vague retention language, so it lands well below the B band. Every quoted mechanism appears verbatim in the snapshot, no certifications were invented, and the empty dealbreaker list is correct because the one adverse practice (selling and sharing for ads) carries a working opt-out.

highlights.label: Full rights menu
highlights.text: The policy grants access, deletion, portability ("in a portable and structured format"), correction, objection, and the right to appeal a refusal, all exercisable via privacy@vanta.com.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers rely on "the EU standard contractual clauses" and self-certification under the EU-U.S., Swiss-U.S., and UK Extension Data Privacy Framework, with the Irish Data Protection Commissioner named as lead supervisory authority.

highlights.label: Sells and shares for ads, with an opt-out
highlights.text: Vanta states it "has sold or shared information (such as identifiers and internet activity information via Cookies) to ad networks," and it provides a Cookie-settings and Global Privacy Control opt-out. That makes it an adverse practice with an opt-out, which counts as a half, not a dealbreaker.

highlights.label: Vague retention and no breach clause
highlights.text: Retention is only "for as long as necessary for the purposes described," with no deletion timeline and no breach-notification commitment, and security rests on "industry-standard technical and organizational measures."

apps.name: Drata
apps.summary: Drata's controller-side privacy notice covers both CCPA and GDPR rights, with a named Data Privacy Framework certification, EU Standard Contractual Clauses, and a listed data protection officer address. It says nothing about AI training of user inputs because the notice expressly excludes Drata's products and customer data, and its retention language stays vague outside the California request windows. The score is at the upper end of C, held back by silent training-data indicators and an unquantified retention period.

highlights.label: Full data subject rights
highlights.text: The notice grants access, deletion, correction, portability, objection, and restriction rights under both the CCPA and GDPR, exercisable through Drata's Privacy Center. The EEA section lists each right explicitly.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers rely on EU Standard Contractual Clauses, the UK Addendum, and adequacy decisions. Drata states it has certified to the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks with the U.S. Department of Commerce.

highlights.label: Vague retention
highlights.text: Personal data is retained as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes for which it was collected. The notice gives no concrete day count or deletion timeline beyond the 30 and 45 day windows that apply only to California rights requests.

highlights.label: Opt-out of sale or sharing
highlights.text: Drata says it has not sold or shared data for money but treats advertising cookie exchanges as a potential sale or share under California law. It offers a Do Not Sell or Share link and honors opt-out preference signals at the browser level.

apps.name: Fireflies.ai
apps.summary: Fireflies.ai earns a strong B on the strength of an explicit commitment not to use personal information for AI model training, a contractual ban on vendors training on the data, and a Zero Data Retention policy for meeting content. It falls short of an A because breach notification is silent and security controls are described only in generic terms, with no named TLS, AES, or RBAC and no SOC 2 or ISO certification.

highlights.label: No model training
highlights.text: The policy states it does not use personal information for AI model training and contractually prohibits vendors from using the information for their own training. A Zero Data Retention policy means meeting content is not used to train internal or external AI models.

highlights.label: Zero retention for meeting content
highlights.text: Account data is deleted within 30 days of account closure, and meeting content covering audio, video, transcripts, and summaries is not stored by any third-party vendor after processing.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: Users can access, correct, delete, port, and object to processing of their personal information, with a named appeals path and a non-retaliation commitment.

highlights.label: Ads sharing with opt-out
highlights.text: The policy discloses that its advertising activities may count as selling or sharing under state laws and provides opt-out routes through the Your Privacy Choices link, a web form, and Global Privacy Control signals.

apps.name: Fathom
apps.summary: Fathom discloses a named training opt-out, a 30-day deletion timeline, broad data-subject rights, Data Privacy Framework-certified international transfers, and an explicit no-sale promise, landing it solidly in the B band. The retention period is only described vaguely as "as long as necessary," and tier differences, output ownership, and shorter AI-log retention are not addressed.

highlights.label: Training opt-out
highlights.text: Fathom trains only its in-house models on de-identified meeting data and states you can opt out in account settings. It also says it does not authorize third parties like OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google to train on your content.

highlights.label: Deletion timeline
highlights.text: The policy commits to using commercially reasonable efforts to delete recordings and personal information within 30 days of an account deletion request.

highlights.label: No sale of content
highlights.text: Fathom states it does not sell Meeting Content Information or attendee information to anyone, and it does not sell or share personal information for third-party direct marketing without permission.

highlights.label: Vague retention anchor
highlights.text: Retention is described only as storing personal information for as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes set out in the policy, with no defined ceiling or shorter AI-log period.

apps.name: Read AI
apps.summary: Read AI discloses a full set of data-subject rights, a dated and versioned policy, and named international-transfer safeguards. It trains on user data by default under legitimate interest with only an opt-out, it shares and sells identifiers, commercial, and inference categories for advertising with an opt-out, and it stays silent on output ownership, breach notification, and a sub-processor list. These gaps place it in the upper-C band.

highlights.label: Trains by default with opt-out
highlights.text: The policy relies on legitimate interest to "train and improve our models within our Services" by default and offers an opt-in Customer Experience Program plus an in-meeting "opt out" command, so training stays on until the user acts.

highlights.label: Shares and sells data for ads with opt-out
highlights.text: Read AI states plainly that "we share and sell" identifiers, commercial, and inference categories to marketing and advertising partners, with opt-out via Cookie Settings, Global Privacy Control, and a web form. Meeting Information is not shared for ads without consent.

highlights.label: Two-year cap on audio and video
highlights.text: Retention is concrete for the core data: "we will store your audio and video information ... but in no case for longer than 2 years." Other data is kept "as long as necessary."

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards and full EU rights
highlights.text: The policy certifies adherence to the EU-U.S., Swiss-U.S., and UK Data Privacy Frameworks and relies on contractual clauses. The Europe section names access, rectification, deletion, restriction, objection, and portability rights.

apps.name: Avoma
apps.summary: Avoma's policy refuses to sell or share data and commits not to train AI or machine learning models on data obtained through Google Workspace APIs. It does not address output ownership, and it ties retention to vague "as long as you use the Service" language with no day-count or deletion timeline.

highlights.label: No AI training on integration data
highlights.text: The policy states that data obtained through Google Workspace APIs is used only to provide and improve the service, and that Avoma does not use this data to develop, improve, or train generalized AI or machine learning models. No clause claims meeting Content is used for training.

highlights.label: Does not sell or share
highlights.text: Avoma states it does not sell, share, rent or lease personally identifiable information, and separately commits that it will not sell or share data as those terms are defined under the CPRA. This covers the strongest dealbreaker.

highlights.label: Vague retention
highlights.text: Retention is tied to keeping information for as long as necessary and as long as you use the Service, with no concrete day-count or deletion timeline for meeting recordings and transcripts.

highlights.label: International transfers covered by DPF
highlights.text: Avoma certifies adherence to the EU-US, UK Extension, and Swiss-US Data Privacy Framework, which is a recognized cross-border transfer safeguard. It also publishes a sub-processor list and a Data Processing Addendum.

apps.name: Guru
apps.summary: Guru's policy gives strong EU and UK data subject rights and maps GDPR legal bases, but it stays silent on AI model training, never discloses it is an AI product, and offers only vague retention language with no timeline.

highlights.label: Full EU and UK rights
highlights.text: The Data Subject Rights section grants access, rectification, restriction, objection, deletion, and portability, each with a described process, plus the right to complain to a supervisory authority.

highlights.label: Silent on AI training
highlights.text: The policy describes data use for operating, analyzing, and improving the Service but never says inputs or Content are used to train models, and it offers no training opt-out.

highlights.label: Vague retention with no timeline
highlights.text: The retention section keeps data as long as necessary for the purpose and deletes it when no longer necessary or upon request, with no day count and no numeric deletion timeline.

highlights.label: Ad tools used and Do Not Track ignored
highlights.text: The policy uses third party tools for ad targeting, remarketing, and conversion tracking, states it has no practices to honor a Do Not Track signal, and describes no ad opt-out.

apps.name: Dashworks
apps.summary: Dashworks discloses a strong set of data-subject rights, a named sub-processor list, transfers backed by the 2021 Standard Contractual Clauses, a 72-hour breach notification commitment, and a named privacy officer. It stays silent on whether user inputs or uploaded content are used to train Dash AI, and it describes retention only in vague terms, keeping data until the account is terminated. That mix of solid rights coverage alongside silence on training and weak retention specificity lands it in the C band.

highlights.label: Full GDPR rights set
highlights.text: The policy spells out access, rectification, objection, erasure, restriction, and portability rights and commits to respond within thirty days, covering all five data-subject indicators.

highlights.label: Named sub-processors and SCCs
highlights.text: A list of third-party service providers names AWS, Stripe, Google Cloud, Segment, and Amplitude, and the EU section states Dashworks complies with the 2021 Standard Contractual Clauses for transfers to the US.

highlights.label: Concrete breach and security detail
highlights.text: Dashworks commits to notify within 72 hours of a personal data breach and names SSL, firewalls, and regular penetration tests as security measures, with EU data transferred and stored in an encrypted format.

highlights.label: Quiet on AI training and retention
highlights.text: The policy never states whether user inputs or uploaded content are used to train Dash AI, and retention is described only as kept until the account is terminated.

apps.name: Hebbia
apps.summary: Hebbia's policy is strong on data-subject rights, sharing transparency, and a clear no-training pledge for customer data. It loses ground on vague retention with no day-count, an absent breach-notification timeline, and silence on output ownership and on shorter AI-log retention. The grade rests on the company's own May 2026 privacy policy at hebbia.com/privacy, with all applicable indicators resolved by a quoted clause or documented silence.

highlights.label: No customer-data training
highlights.text: The policy states that Hebbia does not use Personal Data contained in Customer Data to train or improve generalized or third-party AI or machine learning models, and limits any internal use to debugging, performance monitoring, and feature development.

highlights.label: Does not sell or share
highlights.text: Hebbia states it does not sell personal information for monetary consideration and does not sell or share for cross-context behavioral advertising. It provides a named Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information opt-out in cookie preferences.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: For international transfers the policy relies on the European Commission standard contractual clauses (2021) and the UK International Data Transfer Addendum, and it names a Data Protection Officer along with EU and UK representatives.

highlights.label: Vague retention and no breach timeline
highlights.text: Retention is framed as the duration of the customer relationship plus a period afterward, and prospect data is held until it no longer has business value, with no day-count given. The security section commits to no breach-notification timeframe.

apps.name: Robin AI
apps.summary: Robin AI publishes a compact GDPR-style privacy notice that grants a full set of data-subject rights but stays silent on whether customer documents train AI models, on concrete retention periods, on breach notification, and on security or certification specifics. The grade of 52 (D) survives scrutiny: every claimed quote is present in the snapshot, the adequacy-based transfer safeguard is genuinely named, and no dealbreaker was raised because the policy contains no adverse reservation. The training silence is real silence rather than an adverse train-by-default clause, so it correctly carries no flag.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: The notice grants access, deletion, portability, rectification, and objection. It lets you be provided with a copy of any personal data the company holds about you, require deletion without undue delay, and receive data in machine readable format so it can be ported to a replacement service provider.

highlights.label: Silent on AI training
highlights.text: The policy never states whether uploaded documents, labelled clauses, or accepted edits are used to train AI models, and it offers no training opt-out. Data use is framed only as data analysis, testing and research for the purpose of improving the functionality and usability of products and services or developing new features.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no numbers
highlights.text: Retention is described only as keeping personal data no longer than is necessary to fulfil the purpose for which it was collected. There is no day count, no deletion timeline, and no shorter log-retention window for AI interaction data.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguard but thin security
highlights.text: International transfers rely on a country assessed by the European Commission or an equivalent UK body as ensuring an adequate level of protection. Security is described only as appropriate technical and organisational measures, with no named controls, no breach-notification commitment, and no certification or DPO.

apps.name: Forethought
apps.summary: Forethought's policy lays out a full slate of data-subject rights, per-purpose legal bases, and a plain no-sale commitment, and it names standard contractual clauses for international transfers. It stays silent on whether user data trains AI models and gives only vague retention and security disclosures, which lands it in the middle of the pack.

highlights.label: Full rights catalogue
highlights.text: Section 11 enumerates access, erasure, portability, rectification, objection, and an advertising opt-out, each stated as a concrete right exercisable by contacting the company.

highlights.label: Does not sell data
highlights.text: The U.S. residents section states plainly that Forethought does not rent or sell any personal information, so the advertising-sale indicator scores full with no dealbreaker.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no numbers
highlights.text: Retention is described only as for as long as is necessary for the purposes it was collected, with no day count and a direction to email for the actual periods, so it earns partial credit.

highlights.label: Thin security and accountability
highlights.text: Safeguards are described as reasonable organizational, technical, and physical measures with no named controls, and the policy is silent on breach-notification timeframes and on any certification or named DPO.

apps.name: Ada
apps.summary: Ada lands a solid B. Its Meta supplement states Ada does not use identifiable end-user data to train public AI models and that data sent to LLM providers is covered by zero data retention policies and is not used to train public models. The security section names concrete controls (SOC 2 Type 2, TLS 1.2+, AES-256, role-based access, multi-factor authentication) and a Data Protection Officer, and the policy sets a 90-day post-termination retention window with a typical 30-day turnaround for controller-side deletion requests. It stays silent on data portability, special-category limits, a children's age gate, and breach notification.

highlights.label: No public model training on customer data
highlights.text: The Meta supplement states Ada does not use identifiable end-user data to train public AI models, and that customer data processed by large language model providers is not used to train public models, with those providers operating under zero data retention policies.

highlights.label: Named security stack and SOC 2
highlights.text: Ada lists SOC 2 Type 2 certification, encryption in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES-256), role-based access controls, and multi-factor authentication, plus a Data Protection Officer reachable at dpo@ada.support.

highlights.label: Concrete retention and deletion timelines
highlights.text: After termination Ada retains Meta-channel data for up to 90 days before it is deleted or anonymized, and controller-side deletion requests are handled typically within 30 days.

highlights.label: Gaps on portability, breach, children
highlights.text: The rights section covers access, deletion, correction, and restriction but does not name data portability, and the policy is silent on breach notification, special-category data limits, and a children's age gate.

apps.name: Rogo
apps.summary: Rogo's published privacy policy is a website-visitor notice that explicitly excludes the enterprise AI product, stating that privacy terms for those services live in separate written agreements with each enterprise customer. It includes a strong data-subject rights section but says nothing about AI training, input handling, log retention, or breach notification. The score stays in the D band.

highlights.label: Scope excludes the AI product
highlights.text: The policy covers only website visitor data and says it does not apply to data gathered through the enterprise products and services, whose privacy terms are set out in separate written agreements between Rogo and the enterprise customer. Training and input handling for the financial assistant go unaddressed here.

highlights.label: Full rights menu
highlights.text: The policy grants access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, and portability rights under the EU GDPR, UK DPA 2018, and several US state laws. It sets a one-month response window and names EU and UK GDPR representatives.

highlights.label: Retention left vague
highlights.text: Rogo says it retains personal data for as long as necessary to provide the web site or carry out business and commercial purposes. There is no day-count retention period and no general deletion timeline.

highlights.label: Sale opt-outs but no breach notice
highlights.text: Nevada residents can opt out of the sale of certain personal data, and California residents can block disclosure for direct marketing. The policy names no breach notification process, claims no certifications, and describes only generic security measures across physical, technical, and organizational controls.

apps.name: Eightfold AI
apps.summary: Eightfold's recruiting platform notice earns a B. It grants a full set of data-subject rights, does not sell personal information or use it for behavioral advertising, and names a Subprocessor Notice plus EU-U.S., UK, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Framework transfer safeguards. It also states that personal data is anonymized before any model training, so identifiable inputs are not used to train. Retention stays vague, and the notice says nothing about breach notification.

highlights.label: Trains on anonymized data only
highlights.text: The model-building purpose states that personal data in any training dataset is anonymized by Eightfold with industry-standard techniques before use, so identifiable inputs are not used to train.

highlights.label: Does not sell or share for ads
highlights.text: Eightfold states it does not sell personal information for monetary payment and does not share it for cross-context behavioral advertising. It still offers a Global Privacy Control and an opt-out path for the legal definitions of sale and sharing.

highlights.label: Full recruiting-relevant rights
highlights.text: The notice grants access, correction, deletion, restriction, objection, and portability of automated-means data, plus a right to be free from decisions based solely on automated processing that produce a significant legal effect.

highlights.label: Retention left vague and no breach clause
highlights.text: Retention is described as as long as reasonably necessary, with no day-count or deletion timeline. The security section names firewalls and encryption generically and never commits to breach notification.

apps.name: HireVue
