apps.summary: HireVue's privacy notice gives full data-subject rights, names its international transfer safeguards, and discloses AI use in its sales and meeting recordings. It carves out the candidate and biometric data its hiring platform processes, which separate customer contracts cover. The notice says nothing about model training, sets only a vague retention standard with no day-count, and gives no breach notification timeframe.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: The notice grants access, correction, deletion, and portability, plus the right to restrict or object. It adds a complete CCPA right-to-know, delete, correct, and opt-out section for California residents.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers rely on named mechanisms: European Commission adequacy decisions, Standard Contractual Clauses, the UK International Data Transfer Agreement, and the EU-U.S., UK, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Frameworks.

highlights.label: Silent on the biometric core
highlights.text: Candidate facial and video data sits outside this notice as Customer Candidate Data covered by separate contractual terms. The policy discloses no biometric handling for the data subjects most affected by the hiring product.

highlights.label: Vague retention, no breach clause
highlights.text: Retention is described only as as long as is necessary for the purpose, with no day-count or deletion timeline. The notice states no breach notification commitment or timeframe.

apps.name: ThoughtSpot
apps.summary: ThoughtSpot's privacy statement is strong on data-subject rights, names international transfer safeguards, and makes a clear no-sale commitment. It stays silent on whether user inputs and search-query data feed AI model training, gives only vague retention language with no deletion timeline, and includes no breach-notification clause. That combination lands it in the middle of the C band.

highlights.label: Does not sell data
highlights.text: The statement says plainly that ThoughtSpot does not sell, rent, or trade personal data, and the California section repeats that it does not sell the personal data of California consumers. The selling indicator is full with no dealbreaker.

highlights.label: Strong international transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Transfers out of the EEA, UK, and Switzerland rely on named mechanisms. These are the EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework self-certification with the UK Extension and the Swiss-U.S. framework, the European Commission adequacy decision, and Standard Contractual Clauses.

highlights.label: Full set of data-subject rights
highlights.text: Residents are told they may have the right to access, delete, receive a copy of, object to or restrict, port, and correct their personal data, with a contact channel at privacy@thoughtspot.com. This covers every D2 right.

highlights.label: Vague retention and silent on AI training
highlights.text: Retention is described only as needed to fulfill the purposes, with no day-count and no deletion timeline. The statement never says whether collected search-query or NLP input data feeds model training, so those indicators score half or zero.

apps.name: Sigma Computing
apps.summary: Sigma Computing's controller-side privacy notice covers data-subject rights and international transfer safeguards well. It says nothing about AI model training, and its retention and security disclosures are vague and generic, which places it in the middle of the C band. Because it is a controller document for a BI platform, customer-uploaded data and outputs are governed by a separate Customer Agreement and are outside this policy's scope.

highlights.label: Detailed rights with a response window
highlights.text: Section 5 lists access, correction, deletion, portability, restriction, and opt-out rights. Section 5.3 commits to confirming receipt within 10 business days and responding within 30 to 45 calendar days.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 7 relies on the European Commission Standard Contractual Clauses and the UK International Data Transfer Addendum. Sigma is also self-certified under the EU-U.S., UK Extension, and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Framework.

highlights.label: Vague retention with no timeline
highlights.text: Section 8 keeps data only so long as reasonably necessary for legitimate business purposes, with no day-count and no deletion timeline. This is the weakest part of the notice.

highlights.label: Generic security and no breach clause
highlights.text: Section 9 promises industry standard technical and organizational measures with no named controls such as TLS or AES-256. The notice never states a breach notification commitment.

apps.name: Pigment
apps.summary: Pigment's GDPR-grounded controller policy covers data-subject rights and legal bases for its website, marketing, and prospecting data, but it explicitly excludes the SaaS product and AI Features, which are governed by separate customer contracts. The policy therefore says nothing about whether AI feature inputs train models, about any deletion timeline, or about breach notification, which keeps the score at the bottom of the C band.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: The policy grants access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, portability, and withdrawal of consent, plus the right to lodge a complaint with the CNIL, and names a DPO contact at dpo@pigment.com.

highlights.label: Training disclosure absent
highlights.text: AI Feature inputs and outputs are handled only under separate customer contracts, so the policy never states whether inputs train models and offers no opt-out mechanism. The training-data domain is unscored.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: International transfers outside the EEA rely on EU adequacy decisions or standard contractual clauses established by the European Commission. These are named safeguards for cross-border data flows.

highlights.label: Retention named but no deletion timeline
highlights.text: The legal-basis table sets concrete periods such as three years from the last engagement and the duration of the contract. Even so, the policy gives no specific deletion timeline and no breach notification commitment, and security rests on generic measures like website SSL and monitoring.

apps.name: Jasper
apps.summary: Jasper's privacy policy grants a full slate of GDPR and CCPA data-subject rights, but it says nothing about whether user prompts and generated content train its AI models, so training-data governance goes unscored. The policy explicitly defers output ownership to a separate Terms of Service or EULA, so all four D1 indicators score zero on silence. Retention is described only as "as long as necessary," with no day-count or deletion timeline. International transfers name no specific safeguard, and security is limited to "commercially acceptable means" with no breach commitment or certifications. The CCPA section acknowledges Jasper may sell identifiers, commercial information, and network activity, and it pairs this with a named opt-out.

highlights.label: Silent on AI training
highlights.text: The policy says nothing about whether user prompts or generated content are used to train Jasper's models, and it names no training opt-out, so all four training-data indicators score zero on silence. The policy notes that output ownership lives in the separate Terms of Service or EULA.

highlights.label: Full data-subject rights
highlights.text: The GDPR section grants access with a copy of the data, correction, erasure, portability in a structured machine-readable format, and the right to object to processing, including third-party de-anonymization.

highlights.label: Sells personal information with opt-out
highlights.text: The CCPA section states Jasper may sell and may have sold identifiers, commercial information, and network activity, with a named right to say no to the sale by submitting an opt-out request.

highlights.label: Generic security, no breach commitment
highlights.text: Security is described only as commercially acceptable means, with no named TLS, AES, or access controls, no breach-notification commitment, and no certifications or named DPO.

apps.name: Moveworks
apps.summary: Moveworks publishes a GDPR-style privacy policy with data-subject rights and named international transfer safeguards. It is silent on whether user inputs train AI models, and it gives no concrete retention period.

highlights.label: Training-data silence
highlights.text: The policy never states whether user inputs or content feed AI model training, and it names no training opt-out, so all four training indicators score zero on silence.

highlights.label: Full rights menu
highlights.text: Section 12 grants access, deletion, rectification, portability in a structured machine-readable format, and the right to object. Each is exercisable by email with no discrimination.

highlights.label: Named transfer safeguards
highlights.text: Section 7 relies on EU adequacy decisions for Canada, the US Data Privacy Framework, EU Standard Contractual Clauses, and the UK IDTA, and it confirms active DPF certification.

highlights.label: Vague retention and generic security
highlights.text: Retention is described only as as long as necessary, with no day-count or deletion timeline. Security is current industry standards in transit and at rest, with no named encryption and no breach notice.

apps.name: UiPath
apps.summary: UiPath publishes a GDPR-anchored privacy policy. It handles cloud customer data on a processor basis, names its sub-processors and transfer safeguards, and grants a full set of data-subject rights. Vague retention periods and no named security controls hold it to the B band.

highlights.label: Customer controls training data
highlights.text: For cloud products the customer is the data controller and UiPath the processor. Training is tied to the customer contract for product improvement, and the policy tells users not to put personal or sensitive data into Trial, Community or test versions.

highlights.label: Named sub-processors and transfer safeguards
highlights.text: The sharing section lists specific service providers such as Salesforce, Atlassian, Microsoft 365, AWS and Stripe under contractual agreements. International transfers rely on Standard Contractual Clauses, the UK International Data Transfer Addendum, and EU-U.S. and Swiss-U.S. Data Privacy Framework certification.

highlights.label: No selling or ad-sharing
highlights.text: The policy states it does not sell or share mobile or personal data, and that mobile data will under no circumstances be shared or sold for advertising or promotional use. California residents can opt out of sharing via dpo@uipath.com.

highlights.label: Retention and security stay vague
highlights.text: Most retention is described as a limited period as necessary, with only a few named windows such as Customer Advisory Board data at 18 months, candidate data up to a year, and interview recordings up to 3 months. The security section names no specific controls like TLS or AES, and no breach-notification timeframe is given.
